fieldGuide.analogy: Real-life analogy. Mostly on levels 1-2; often absent later.
fieldGuide.code: Code example: language, lines (array of strings, join with \n), and explanation.
fieldGuide.diagram: Schema: type (mermaid), lines (mermaid source), caption. Render with mermaid.js.
fieldGuide.exercises: Practice tasks. type is one of task | fix-bug | predict | quiz; each has a prompt, optional starter/hint, and a solution (lines and/or explanation).
fieldGuide.docs: Link to the official react.dev docs.

exerciseTypes.task: Write code to satisfy the prompt; solution.lines shows a reference answer.
exerciseTypes.fix-bug: starter contains broken code; fix it. solution.lines is the corrected version.
exerciseTypes.predict: Predict the output/behavior; solution.explanation gives the answer.
exerciseTypes.quiz: Conceptual question; solution.explanation gives the answer.

legend.note: analogy, code, diagram, commonMistakes are optional and added where they help. ids are preserved from your roadmap, so localStorage progress keeps working.

concepts.title: What React is: declarative UI and the component model
concepts.summary: React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces out of small, independent pieces called components. You describe HOW the screen should look for the current data, and React makes the real page match that description.
concepts.explanation: The core idea of React is being declarative. Instead of writing manual steps like 'find this element, change its text, add a class' (the imperative style, as in jQuery), you just describe the result you want for a given state. When the data changes, React figures out the difference and updates only what actually changed. This makes code predictable: the UI is a function of state, written UI = f(state). The whole app is built from components that nest inside one another and get reused, like LEGO bricks.
concepts.analogy: Declarative is like ordering at a restaurant: you say 'I want a cappuccino' instead of explaining every step to the cook. Imperative is standing at the stove and doing every step yourself.
keyPoints: Declarative: you describe the final look of the UI, not step-by-step DOM changes.
UI = f(state): the same data always produces the same UI.
Component model: the interface is composed of small, reusable pieces.
React decides what to update in the real DOM, and when.

code.explanation: ProductCard is a component (a plain function returning JSX). It describes how a product card looks for the data in product. We never manually create elements or insert text - we only describe the result.

diagram.caption: Data flow in React: an event changes state, React rebuilds the UI description, and only the needed parts of the screen update.

commonMistakes: Treating React like jQuery and changing the DOM by hand (document.querySelector...). In React, the DOM is almost always updated by React.
Thinking a component is HTML. It is really a function that returns a description of UI.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence best describes the declarative style?

solution.explanation: 'I describe what the UI should look like for the current data, and the library updates the DOM.' Declarative = describe the result; imperative = describe each manual step.

exercises.prompt: Write a component UserBadge that displays a hard-coded name and city in two separate lines.

solution.explanation: A component is just a function returning JSX. The data lives in a variable and is read inside {}.

checkYourself: How does the declarative approach differ from the imperative one?
What does the formula UI = f(state) mean?

concepts.title: JSX: expressions in {}, attributes, single root element, self-closing tags
concepts.summary: JSX is a syntax extension that lets you write HTML-like markup directly inside JavaScript. The browser does not understand JSX: a build tool (e.g. Babel) compiles it into plain function calls.
concepts.explanation: JSX only looks like HTML, but it is JavaScript. Each tag compiles into a React.createElement(...) call that returns a plain object - a description of the element. So the rules of JavaScript apply inside JSX, not the rules of HTML: inside curly braces {} you can put any expression, some attributes have different names, and a component must return a single root element.
concepts.analogy: JSX is like a translator between markup that is comfortable for humans and the objects React actually works with.
keyPoints: Inside {} you put expressions (values), not statements: {user.name}, {2 + 2}, {isOpen && <Modal />}. You cannot put if or for there directly.
Attributes are camelCase and sometimes renamed: className instead of class, htmlFor instead of for, onClick instead of onclick.
Single root element: if you need several, wrap them in a <div> or an empty Fragment <>...</>.

Empty tags self-close: ,
, .

code.explanation:
All four rules at once: {name} is an expression in braces; className is the JSX attribute; conditional render via &&; a self-closed ; and everything wrapped in one root
.

diagram.caption: JSX path to the screen: JSX -> Babel -> React.createElement -> element object -> real DOM.

commonMistakes: Writing class instead of className (a habit from HTML).
Returning two sibling elements without a wrapper - that is an error. Fix: a Fragment <>...</>.
Putting if or for directly inside {} - only expressions work there. Use the ternary operator or && for conditions.

Forgetting to self-close ,
, .

exercises.prompt: This component does not compile. Find and fix all the JSX mistakes.
exercises.hint: There are three problems: a wrong attribute name, an unclosed tag, and two root elements.

solution.explanation: class -> className, -> , and the two siblings are wrapped in a Fragment to satisfy the single-root rule.

exercises.prompt:
What renders:

{2 + 2}

versus

2 + 2

?

solution.explanation: The first renders '4' (the braces evaluate the expression). The second renders the literal text '2 + 2', because outside braces it is plain text.

checkYourself: Why do we write className instead of class in JSX?
What can and cannot go inside curly braces {}?
How do you return several elements without an extra <div>?

concepts.title: Rendering the root: createRoot and root.render
concepts.summary: A React app needs one entry point in the real DOM. createRoot connects React to a DOM node, and root.render(<App />) tells React to render your top component into it.
concepts.explanation:
Your HTML page usually has a single empty element like
. In your JavaScript entry file you call createRoot(domNode) once to create a React root tied to that element, then call root.render(). From that point React owns everything inside that node and updates it for you. You normally do this only once, at app startup.
concepts.analogy: createRoot is like handing React the keys to one room (the root div). After that, React decorates and rearranges that room; you stop touching it manually.
keyPoints: createRoot comes from 'react-dom/client' (React 18+).
You pass it a real DOM node, usually document.getElementById('root').
root.render(<App />) mounts your component tree.
This setup runs once; after that, state changes drive all updates.

code.explanation: We grab the #root element, create a React root from it, and render <App /> inside. React now controls that subtree.

commonMistakes: Using the old ReactDOM.render (deprecated in React 18). Use createRoot instead.
Calling createRoot more than once on the same node.
Forgetting that the target <div id="root"> must exist in the HTML before this code runs.

exercises.prompt:
Given a page with
, write the entry code that renders a component called Dashboard into it.

solution.explanation: Same pattern, just targeting #app and rendering Dashboard.

checkYourself: Where does createRoot come from, and what do you pass it?
How many times do you usually call createRoot in an app?

concepts.title: StrictMode: what it does and why it double-invokes in development
concepts.summary: StrictMode is a development-only wrapper that helps you catch bugs early. It intentionally double-invokes certain functions in development to surface impure code and missing effect cleanups.
concepts.explanation: You wrap part (or all) of your tree in <StrictMode>. It does not render any visible UI and does nothing in production. In development it deliberately runs component bodies and certain hooks twice, and mounts-unmounts-remounts effects once extra. This is a stress test: if your component is pure and your effects clean up properly, the double run is harmless. If you see things break or values double, that is a real bug StrictMode is exposing, not a React bug.
concepts.analogy: StrictMode is like a coach making you run a drill twice to check your form. It is annoying on purpose - if you stumble the second time, your technique needed fixing.
keyPoints: Development-only: it has zero effect in production builds.
Double-invokes component render functions and many hooks to reveal impurity.
Runs effect setup+cleanup an extra time to reveal missing cleanup.
It never renders visible markup itself.

code.explanation: Wrapping <App /> in <StrictMode> turns on the extra development checks for the whole tree.

commonMistakes: Thinking the double console.log is a bug in your code or in React - in development it is expected under StrictMode.
Trying to 'fix' double-invocation by removing StrictMode instead of fixing the impure code it revealed.
Putting side effects directly in the component body (they then run twice) instead of inside useEffect.

exercises.prompt: In development with StrictMode, a component body has console.log('render'). How many times does it print on first mount, and does that happen in production?

solution.explanation: It prints twice in development because StrictMode double-invokes the render function. In production it prints once - StrictMode does nothing there.

checkYourself: Does StrictMode affect the production build?
Why does StrictMode mount and unmount effects an extra time?

concepts.title: Function components and composition
concepts.summary: A function component is a JavaScript function whose name is Capitalized and which returns JSX. Composition means building bigger components by nesting smaller ones.
concepts.explanation: In modern React, components are functions. The name MUST start with a capital letter (Header, not header) - that is how React tells your components apart from regular HTML tags. A component returns JSX, and you use it like a tag: <Header />. You build complex UIs by composing: a Page contains a Header, a Sidebar, and a Content, each of which may contain still smaller components. This keeps each piece small, focused, and reusable.
concepts.analogy: Components compose like functions calling functions, or like nesting boxes: a big box (Page) holds smaller labeled boxes (Header, Content), which hold even smaller ones.
keyPoints: Component names start with a capital letter; lowercase names are treated as DOM tags.
A component returns JSX (or null).
Use a component as a tag: <MyComponent />.
Composition: nest small components to build larger ones - prefer this over giant components.

code.explanation: Profile composes Avatar by using it as a tag. Small components combine into bigger ones.

commonMistakes: Naming a component in lowercase (function avatar) - React will treat <avatar /> as an unknown HTML tag.
Defining one component inside another component's body (it gets recreated every render and loses state). Define components at the top level.
Returning multiple root elements without a Fragment.

exercises.prompt: Create two components: Logo (renders a heading 'MyApp') and Navbar that composes Logo plus a 'Home' link.

solution.explanation: Navbar reuses Logo by composition - the essence of building React UIs.

exercises.prompt: Why does <card /> render nothing useful here, and how do you fix it?

solution.explanation: Lowercase names are treated as HTML tags. Capitalize the component name and its usage.

checkYourself: Why must component names be capitalized?
What does it mean to compose components?

concepts.title: Props: passing, reading, destructuring, and default values
concepts.summary: Props are inputs to a component, passed like HTML attributes. The component receives them as a single object and can read, destructure, and give them default values.
concepts.explanation: Props let a parent configure a child. You pass them as attributes: <Greeting name="Illia" />. Inside the child you receive one object (conventionally called props) and read props.name. It is cleaner to destructure in the parameter list: function Greeting({ name }). You can also set defaults with regular JavaScript default values: function Greeting({ name = 'Guest' }). Strings go in quotes; anything else (numbers, booleans, objects, arrays, JSX) goes in braces: count={5}, active={true}, items={list}.
concepts.analogy: Props are like arguments to a function, or settings on a device: the same TV (component) behaves differently depending on the channel and volume (props) you give it.
keyPoints: Pass props as attributes: <Comp a="text" b={42} c={obj} />.
A component receives all props as one object.
Destructure for readability: function Comp({ a, b }).
Defaults via JS default values: function Comp({ size = 'md' }).
Use {} for non-string values; quotes only for plain strings.

code.explanation: Greeting destructures name and excited with defaults. The first usage passes both; the second relies on defaults ('Guest', not excited).

diagram.caption: Props flow one way: from parent down to child.

commonMistakes: Passing a number or boolean in quotes: count="5" gives the STRING '5'. Use count={5}.
Forgetting that props are read inside the child as one object unless you destructure.
Trying to change a prop inside the child (props are read-only - see the next concept).

exercises.prompt: Write a Price component that takes amount (number) and currency (string, default 'USD') and renders like '1200 USD'.

solution.explanation: amount is passed in braces (a number), currency has a default.

exercises.prompt: What does <Box size="3" /> give for `size` inside, a number or a string? And <Box size={3} />?

solution.explanation: size="3" gives the string '3'. size={3} gives the number 3. Quotes always mean a string.

checkYourself: How do you pass a boolean prop?
How do you give a prop a default value?

concepts.title: Props are read-only (immutability)
concepts.summary: A component must never modify its own props. Props flow down from the parent and are treated as read-only inside the child.
concepts.explanation: React's data flow is one-directional: the parent owns the data and passes it down as props; the child only reads them. If a child mutates props (props.name = 'X'), it breaks React's assumptions and leads to confusing bugs, because the parent did not authorize that change. If a component needs to change something over time, that something must be STATE (owned by the component), not a prop. If a child needs to influence a parent, the parent passes down a callback function as a prop, and the child calls it.
concepts.analogy: Props are like a read-only memo from your manager. You can act on it, but you do not edit the manager's original document. If something needs changing, you ask the manager (call a callback) or keep your own notes (state).
keyPoints: Never assign to props inside a component.
Data flows one way: parent -> child.
Need a value that changes over time? Use state, not a prop.
Need to affect the parent? The parent passes a callback prop the child can call.

lines: // RIGHT: read props, derive a new value if needed
code.explanation: Good never mutates the prop; it derives a new local value. Bad mutates shared data and invites bugs.

commonMistakes: Mutating an object or array received via props.
Confusing 'a value that changes' (should be state) with 'configuration from the parent' (props).
Reaching into the parent's data instead of asking via a callback prop.

exercises.prompt: A child needs to update a counter shown by the parent. What is the correct approach?

solution.explanation: The parent holds the counter in state and passes down a callback (e.g. onIncrement). The child calls that callback; it never edits the prop directly.

checkYourself: Why are props read-only?
If a value must change over time, where should it live?

concepts.title: The children prop
concepts.summary: children is a special prop that holds whatever JSX you nest between a component's opening and closing tags. It lets components wrap arbitrary content.
concepts.explanation: When you write <Card>some content</Card>, the 'some content' is passed to Card as props.children. This is how you make flexible container components - a Card, Modal, or Layout that does not know in advance what it will contain. You render it by placing {children} wherever the nested content should appear. This is the foundation of composition: the wrapper provides structure and styling, the caller provides the content.
concepts.analogy: children is like a picture frame: the frame (Card) is fixed, but you can put any photo (children) inside it.
keyPoints: Anything between <Comp> and </Comp> becomes props.children.
Render it with {children}.
Enables generic wrappers (Card, Layout, Modal) that hold arbitrary content.
children can be text, one element, many elements, or even components.

code.explanation: Card renders whatever is nested inside it via {children}. The same Card can wrap any content.

diagram.caption: Nested JSX arrives as props.children and is rendered wherever {children} appears.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to actually render {children} - then nested content disappears.
Passing content as a custom prop when children would be more natural.
Expecting children to always be a single element (it can be an array).

exercises.prompt: Build a Panel component that shows a fixed header 'Info' and renders any nested children below it.

solution.explanation: The header is fixed; {children} renders whatever the caller nests inside <Panel>...</Panel>.

checkYourself: What ends up in props.children?
Why is children useful for wrapper components?

concepts.title: Conditional rendering: if, ternary, &&, early return, returning null
concepts.summary: You decide what to show using plain JavaScript: an if/early return before the JSX, or a ternary and && inside the JSX. Returning null renders nothing.
concepts.explanation: Because JSX is just JavaScript, you use normal logic to render conditionally. Outside the return you can use if statements and early returns. Inside JSX, where only expressions are allowed, you use the ternary (cond ? a : b) to choose between two options, or && (cond && <X />) to render something or nothing. To render nothing at all, return null. Be careful with &&: a falsy left side like 0 will actually render '0', so prefer a real boolean.
concepts.analogy: It is like a bouncer at a door: if the condition is met, the content gets in; otherwise it does not. Same JavaScript logic you already know.
keyPoints: Outside JSX: use if and early return.
Inside JSX: use ternary cond ? a : b, or cond && <X /> for show/hide.
Return null to render nothing.
Gotcha: count && <X /> renders '0' when count is 0; use count > 0 && <X />.

code.explanation: Three styles together: early returns for loading/empty, && to guard a line, and a ternary to choose text.

commonMistakes: Using count && <X /> with a numeric count of 0, which renders a stray '0'.
Trying to put a full if statement inside {} (only expressions allowed there).
Forgetting that returning undefined is not allowed - return null to render nothing.

exercises.prompt: When cartCount is 0, this shows a stray '0' on screen. Fix it.

solution.explanation: 0 is falsy but still renders as text. Convert the left side to a real boolean with cartCount > 0.

exercises.prompt: Write a Badge that returns null when `unread` is 0, otherwise shows the number.

solution.explanation: Early return null hides the badge entirely when there is nothing to show.

checkYourself: Why can the && pattern accidentally render '0'?
How do you render nothing from a component?

concepts.title: Rendering lists with .map()
concepts.summary: To render a list, transform an array of data into an array of JSX elements using .map(). React renders arrays of elements directly.
concepts.explanation: There is no special loop syntax in JSX. You take your data array and call .map() to turn each item into an element, producing an array of JSX that React renders in order. Each element in such a list needs a key (covered next) so React can track items efficiently. .map() is preferred over a for loop here because it is an expression and returns the new array inline.
concepts.analogy: It is like a mail merge: one template applied to each row of data produces one output per row.
keyPoints: Use array.map(item => <El />) to produce a list of elements.
JSX has no loop keyword; .map() is the idiom.
Each rendered list item needs a key prop.
You can map over objects' arrays, filter first, then map, etc.

code.explanation: todos.map turns each todo object into an <li>. The result is an array of elements React renders inside the <ul>.

diagram.caption: An array of data becomes an array of elements via .map(), which React renders in order.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to return the element from .map() (using {} without return).
Omitting the key prop on list items.
Trying to use a for loop directly inside JSX braces.

exercises.prompt: Given an array users (each {id, name}), render a <ul> with one <li> per name.

solution.explanation: Map each user to an <li>, keyed by the stable id.

exercises.prompt: This list renders nothing. Why?

solution.explanation: Using {} as the arrow body needs an explicit return. Switching to () (or adding return) makes the arrow return the element.

checkYourself: Why use .map() instead of a for loop in JSX?
What must each mapped element include?

concepts.title: Keys and why they matter
concepts.summary: A key is a stable, unique string you give each item in a list. React uses keys to match items across renders so it can update, reorder, or remove them correctly.
concepts.explanation: When a list re-renders, React needs to know which new element corresponds to which old one. Keys provide that identity. With good keys (usually a stable id from your data), React can move, update, and delete items efficiently and preserve their state. Using the array index as a key is risky: if items are reordered, inserted, or removed, the indexes shift and React may associate the wrong state with the wrong item. Keys must be unique among siblings and stable across renders - never use Math.random().
concepts.analogy: Keys are like name tags at a conference. If everyone keeps their tag, the organizer can track each person even when the room rearranges. Index-as-key is like tagging people by their current seat number - shuffle the seats and the tags now point to the wrong people.
keyPoints: Key = stable unique identity for a list item.
Lets React match old and new items to update/reorder/remove correctly.
Prefer a stable data id over the array index.
Keys must be unique among siblings and stable; never Math.random().
Keys are not passed to the component as a prop - they are for React.

lines: // Risky: index as key (problems if the list reorders or changes)
code.explanation: The first uses a stable id, so item identity survives reordering. The second uses index, which can mis-associate state when the list changes.

commonMistakes: Using the array index as key for lists that can reorder, insert, or delete.
Using Math.random() (a new key every render destroys reuse).
Expecting to read key inside the component - it is not a normal prop.

exercises.prompt: A list of inputs uses key={index}. You delete the first item. Why might the wrong input value remain on screen?

solution.explanation: After deletion, indexes shift up. React matches by index, so the state (input value) of old index 1 now attaches to the element that is now index 0, showing a value that belonged to a different item. A stable id key avoids this.

exercises.prompt: Is key passed to the child component as a prop you can read?

solution.explanation: No. key is consumed by React to track list identity; it is not available as props.key inside the child. If you also need that value, pass it again under a different prop name.

checkYourself: What problem do keys solve?
Why is index-as-key risky?

concepts.title: Fragment and the <>...</> shorthand
concepts.summary: A Fragment lets you return multiple elements without adding an extra wrapper node to the DOM. The shorthand syntax is <>...</>.
concepts.explanation: JSX requires a single root, but you do not always want a real wrapper <div> (it can break layouts like fl/grid or tables). A Fragment groups children without rendering any extra DOM element. Use the shorthand <>...</> in most cases. When you render a list of fragments and each needs a key, use the explicit form <Fragment key={id}>...</Fragment>, because the shorthand cannot take a key.
concepts.analogy: A Fragment is like an invisible paperclip: it holds several pages together without adding a visible cover sheet.
keyPoints: Fragment groups elements without adding a DOM node.
Shorthand: <>...</>.
Use <Fragment key={...}> when you need a key (the shorthand cannot take props).
Helps avoid 'div soup' and keeps valid HTML (e.g. table rows).

code.explanation: Cells uses the shorthand to return two <td> without a wrapper. List needs keys, so it uses the explicit Fragment.

commonMistakes: Wrapping everything in <div> when no wrapper is needed, breaking layouts.
Trying to put a key on the shorthand <> (not allowed) instead of <Fragment key=...>.
Importing Fragment unnecessarily when the shorthand would do.

exercises.prompt: Return a label and an input from a component without adding any wrapper DOM element.

solution.explanation: The shorthand Fragment groups both elements with no extra DOM node.

checkYourself: Why use a Fragment instead of a <div>?
When must you use <Fragment> instead of the <> shorthand?

concepts.title: State with useState; state as a snapshot
concepts.summary: useState gives a component memory: a value that persists across renders and, when changed, triggers a re-render. Within a single render, state is a fixed snapshot.
concepts.explanation: Call useState(initial) to get a pair: the current value and a setter function, via array destructuring: const [count, setCount] = useState(0). Calling setCount(value) asks React to re-render with the new value. The key mental model: during one render, state is a snapshot - a constant. The variable count does not change mid-render even after you call setCount; you will see the new value on the NEXT render. This is why reading state right after setting it shows the old value.
concepts.analogy: A render is like a photograph: state is frozen in that photo. Calling the setter does not edit the current photo - it schedules a new photo to be taken with updated values.
keyPoints: const [value, setValue] = useState(initial).
The setter triggers a re-render.
State is constant within a single render (a snapshot).
You see updated state on the next render, not immediately after setting.

code.explanation: After setCount, the console still shows the old count because this render's snapshot does not change. The UI updates on the next render.

diagram.caption: Setting state schedules a re-render; the new value appears on the next render, not synchronously.

commonMistakes: Expecting state to update immediately after calling the setter in the same render.
Mutating the state variable directly instead of calling the setter.
Declaring useState inside a condition or loop (breaks the Rules of Hooks).

exercises.prompt: Inside one click handler you call setCount(count + 1) three times (count starts at 0). What is count after the click, and why?

solution.explanation: It becomes 1, not 3. All three calls read the same snapshot (count = 0), so each sets 0 + 1. To add 3, use the updater form setCount(c => c + 1) three times (next concept).

exercises.prompt: Build a Toggle button that switches its label between 'On' and 'Off' using a boolean state.

solution.explanation: The boolean flips on click; the label reflects the current snapshot.

checkYourself: Why does logging state right after setting it show the old value?
What two things does useState return?

concepts.title: Updating state with an updater function
concepts.summary: When the next state depends on the previous state, pass a function to the setter: setCount(prev => prev + 1). React calls it with the latest pending value.
concepts.explanation: Because state is a snapshot, calling setCount(count + 1) several times in one event all use the same stale count. The fix is the updater function: setCount(c => c + 1). React queues these updaters and applies them in order, each receiving the result of the previous one. Use the updater form whenever the new value is computed from the old value (counters, toggles, appending to arrays), and especially when you call the setter multiple times or inside async callbacks.
concepts.analogy: Direct value setting is like everyone writing the same final score on a sticky note based on the score they saw at the start. The updater function is like passing one notebook around where each person adds to whatever the previous person wrote.
keyPoints: Updater form: setX(prev => next).
React applies queued updaters in order, each seeing the latest value.
Use it whenever next state depends on previous state.
Essential when setting multiple times in one handler or inside async code.

code.explanation: Each updater receives the latest queued value, so three calls add three. With setCount(count + 1) it would only add one.

commonMistakes: Using setCount(count + 1) multiple times and expecting cumulative results.
Reading state inside setTimeout/async and getting a stale value instead of using the updater.
Putting side effects inside the updater function (it should be pure).

exercises.prompt: This 'add two' button only ever adds one. Fix it.

lines: const addTwo = () => { setN((p) => p + 1); setN((p) => p + 1); };
solution.explanation: Both direct calls read the same snapshot. The updater form chains off the latest pending value, so it adds two.

checkYourself: When should you prefer the updater function over a direct value?
Why do multiple direct setters in one handler not stack?

concepts.title: Immutability of objects and arrays in state
concepts.summary: Never mutate objects or arrays held in state. Create a new copy with the change applied and pass that to the setter, so React detects the update.
concepts.explanation: React decides whether to re-render by comparing the new state value to the old by reference. If you mutate an existing object/array (obj.x = 1 or arr.push(...)), the reference stays the same and React may not re-render, or you get subtle bugs. Instead, produce a NEW object/array: use the spread operator { ...obj, x: 1 }, or array methods that return new arrays (map, filter, concat, slice, plus [...arr, item]). Avoid mutating methods (push, pop, splice, sort in place) on state arrays.
concepts.analogy: Editing state in place is like correcting a printed document with a pencil but never reprinting it - the system still sees the 'old' copy. Immutability is like printing a fresh, corrected copy each time.
keyPoints: React compares by reference; same reference can mean 'no change'.
Objects: copy with spread { ...obj, key: value }.
Arrays: use map/filter/concat or [...arr, item], not push/splice.
Nested updates: copy each level you change.

code.explanation: Each pattern produces a brand-new object or array so React sees a new reference and re-renders correctly.

commonMistakes: Using arr.push / arr.splice / obj.key = ... on state values.
Copying only the top level but mutating a nested object inside.
Calling sort() directly on a state array (it mutates) instead of [...arr].sort().

exercises.prompt: Adding a todo does not re-render. Fix the mutation.

solution.explanation: push mutates the same array, so the reference is unchanged. Spreading into a new array gives React a new reference.

exercises.prompt: Given state form = { name, email }, write a setter call that updates only email to 'a@b.com'.

solution.explanation: Spread keeps name; only email is overwritten in the new object.

checkYourself: Why can mutating state silently fail to re-render?
Which array methods are safe for state updates?

concepts.title: Events: onClick, onChange, the event object, passing handlers
concepts.summary: You handle DOM events with camelCase props like onClick and onChange, passing a function. React gives your handler a synthetic event object that works the same across browsers.
concepts.explanation: Attach handlers as props: onClick={handleClick}. Pass the function itself, not its call (onClick={handleClick}, not onClick={handleClick()}). The handler receives a SyntheticEvent - a cross-browser wrapper around the native event with the usual API (e.target, e.preventDefault(), e.stopPropagation()). To pass arguments, wrap in an inline arrow: onClick={() => handleDelete(id)}. You can also pass handlers down to children as props so a child button can notify its parent.
concepts.analogy: Passing handleClick vs handleClick() is like giving someone a recipe versus cooking the dish immediately. React wants the recipe (the function) to run later when the event happens.
keyPoints: Handlers are camelCase props: onClick, onChange, onSubmit.
Pass the function, not its result: onClick={fn}, not onClick={fn()}.
The handler gets a SyntheticEvent (e.target, e.preventDefault(), ...).
Pass arguments with an arrow wrapper: onClick={() => fn(id)}.
Handlers can be passed to children as props.

code.explanation: handleChange reads e.target.value. The delete button passes an argument via an arrow wrapper so it is not called immediately.

commonMistakes: Writing onClick={handleClick()} which calls it during render instead of on click.
Forgetting the arrow wrapper when passing arguments.
Lowercasing event names (onclick) - React expects onClick.

exercises.prompt: The alert fires immediately on render, not on click. Fix it.

solution.explanation: alert('hi') runs during render. Wrapping it in an arrow defers it until the click.

exercises.prompt: Write an input whose onChange stores the typed value into a state variable text.

solution.explanation: e.target.value is the current text; the setter updates state on every keystroke.

checkYourself: What is the difference between onClick={fn} and onClick={fn()}?
How do you pass an argument to an event handler?

concepts.title: Controlled forms: value + onChange and handling submit
concepts.summary: In a controlled input, React state is the single source of truth: the input's value comes from state, and onChange updates that state. On submit you read state and call preventDefault.
concepts.explanation: A controlled component ties an input's displayed value to state (value={text}) and updates state on every change (onChange={e => setText(e.target.value)}). This means the UI and state never drift apart, and you can validate, transform, or disable based on the current value. For forms, handle onSubmit on the <form>, call e.preventDefault() to stop the browser's full-page reload, then use the state values. Checkboxes use checked instead of value; selects also use value.
concepts.analogy: A controlled input is like a thermostat display wired to the actual setting: the number you see always reflects the real value, and changing it goes through the same control.
keyPoints: Controlled input: value={state} + onChange={e => setState(e.target.value)}.
State is the single source of truth for the field.
Handle onSubmit on <form>; call e.preventDefault() to avoid reload.
Checkbox uses checked (a boolean); select uses value.
Lets you validate/transform/disable based on current value.

code.explanation: The input is controlled by email state. Submit prevents the default reload and reads the state value.

diagram.caption: Controlled input loop: typing updates state, state feeds back into the input's value.

commonMistakes: Forgetting e.preventDefault(), so the page reloads on submit.
Setting value without onChange, which makes the input read-only.
Using value instead of checked for checkboxes.

exercises.prompt: Typing in this input does nothing. Fix it.

solution.explanation: A value without onChange locks the field. Adding onChange lets state update on each keystroke.

exercises.prompt: Make a controlled checkbox bound to a boolean state `agree`.

solution.explanation: Checkboxes use checked (boolean) and read e.target.checked, not value.

checkYourself: Why call e.preventDefault() on submit?
What makes an input 'controlled'?

concepts.title: useState in depth: functional updates, lazy initialization, multiple states
concepts.summary: Beyond the basics, useState supports functional updates (prev => next), lazy initialization (pass a function to compute the initial value once), and using several independent state variables in one component.
concepts.explanation: Three deeper points. (1) Functional updates: setX(prev => next) when the new value depends on the old, so you always use the latest queued value. (2) Lazy initialization: if the initial value is expensive to compute, pass a function: useState(() => expensive()). React calls it only on the first render, not on every render. Note: useState(expensive()) would RUN expensive() every render and throw the result away. (3) Multiple states: it is normal to call useState several times for unrelated values; keep separate concerns separate, and group only values that change together.
concepts.analogy: Lazy init is like only assembling the furniture the first day you move in, not re-buying it every morning. Functional updates are the notebook-passed-around idea again.
keyPoints: Functional update: setX(prev => next) for previous-dependent changes.
Lazy init: useState(() => compute()) runs compute once, on first render.
useState(compute()) re-runs compute every render - avoid for expensive work.
Multiple useState calls are fine; split unrelated state, group related state.

lines: // lazy init: parse runs only on first render
code.explanation: doc uses lazy init (parse once) and a functional update. dirty is a separate state since it changes for a different reason.

commonMistakes: Calling useState(expensive()) instead of useState(() => expensive()), re-running the work each render.
Cramming unrelated values into one object state, causing extra copying.
Forgetting the functional form when chaining dependent updates.

exercises.prompt: This recomputes a heavy initial value on every render. Make it run once.

solution.explanation: Passing a function defers and memoizes the initializer: React only calls it on the first render.

exercises.prompt: You have a form with name, email, and age that always change separately. One big object state or three states?

solution.explanation: Three separate states are cleaner here since the fields change independently and you avoid spreading the whole object on each edit. Group into one object only when values truly change together.

checkYourself: What is the difference between useState(f()) and useState(() => f())?
When should you split state into multiple variables?

concepts.title: Mental model: trigger to render to commit (render phases)
concepts.summary: An update goes through three steps: a trigger (initial mount or a state change) causes React to render (call your components to compute the UI), then commit (apply the minimal changes to the real DOM).
concepts.explanation: Rendering in React means calling your component functions to produce a description of the UI - it does NOT mean touching the DOM yet. The flow is: (1) Trigger - the first render, or a setState call. (2) Render - React calls your components; this should be pure (no side effects, just compute JSX). For updates, React compares the new output with the previous to find what changed. (3) Commit - React applies only the necessary DOM mutations. After commit, the browser paints. Effects (useEffect) run after the commit. Understanding these phases explains why rendering must be pure and why effects, not render bodies, are where you do side effects.
concepts.analogy: Render is drawing up blueprints; commit is the construction crew making only the changes the new blueprint requires. Drawing blueprints should not start pouring concrete (no side effects during render).
keyPoints: Trigger: first mount or a state update.
Render: React calls components to compute JSX; must be pure.
Commit: React applies the minimal DOM changes.
Browser paints after commit; effects run after that.
Rendering != updating the DOM.

lines: T[Trigger: mount or setState] --> R[Render: call components]
diagram.caption: The three phases: trigger, render (pure), commit; then paint and effects.

commonMistakes: Believing a render immediately changes the DOM.
Doing side effects (fetch, subscriptions, DOM writes) during render instead of in effects.
Assuming effects run before paint (they run after commit).

exercises.prompt: Put these in order: commit, trigger, render. And where do effects run relative to them?

solution.explanation: Order: trigger -> render -> commit. Effects run after commit (after the browser paints). That is why render must stay pure and effects hold the side effects.

checkYourself: Does 'render' mean updating the DOM?
Why must the render phase be pure?

concepts.title: How React queues state updates
concepts.summary: React batches multiple state updates that happen in the same event into a single re-render. Queued updater functions are applied in order before the next render.
concepts.explanation: When you call several setters inside one event handler, React does not re-render after each one. It batches them and re-renders once with the final result - this is more efficient and avoids flickering. If you pass plain values, the last write per state wins. If you pass updater functions, React queues them and applies them in sequence, each seeing the previous result. Since React 18, batching also applies inside promises, setTimeout, and native event handlers (automatic batching). Knowing this explains why you do not see intermediate states and why updater functions matter for dependent updates.
concepts.analogy: It is like a barista taking your whole order before making anything, instead of walking to the machine after each item. They process the queue, then serve once.
keyPoints: Multiple setters in one event are batched into one re-render.
Plain values: last set per state wins.
Updater functions: queued and applied in order.
React 18 batches in promises/timeouts/native handlers too (automatic batching).
You never see the intermediate renders from a batch.

lines: setN(n + 1); // also value 1 -> result is 1, ONE re-render
setN((p) => p + 1); // chains -> result is 2, ONE re-render
code.explanation: Both handlers cause a single re-render. handle ends at 1 (both read the same snapshot); handleFixed ends at 2 (updaters chain).

commonMistakes: Expecting a re-render after each setter call in the same event.
Assuming pre-18 behavior where async callbacks did not batch.
Mixing value and updater forms and being surprised by the result.

exercises.prompt: Inside one click: setFlag(true); setCount(c => c + 1); setName('A'). How many re-renders?

solution.explanation: One re-render. React batches all three updates in the same event and renders once with the combined result.

checkYourself: Why does React batch updates within an event?
What changed about batching in React 18?

concepts.title: useEffect: synchronizing with external systems
concepts.summary: useEffect runs code after render to synchronize your component with systems outside React: network requests, subscriptions, timers, browser APIs, and manual DOM work. It is not for transforming data during render.
concepts.explanation: Effects let a component reach outside React's world. You call useEffect(setup, deps). After React commits the render to the DOM, it runs your setup function. Use it to start a subscription, set up a timer, fetch data, or sync with a non-React widget. The mental shift: effects are about synchronization with an external system, not about reacting to clicks (that is event handlers) or computing values from props/state (that is plain render logic). If your 'effect' does not touch anything external, you probably do not need it.
concepts.analogy: An effect is like setting up a phone line to an outside office after you have moved into your room (rendered). You connect to the external system once you are settled.
keyPoints: useEffect(setup, deps) runs after commit.
Purpose: synchronize with external systems (network, subscriptions, timers, DOM, third-party widgets).
Not for event responses (use handlers) or derived data (use render logic).
Pairs with a dependency array and an optional cleanup.

code.explanation: The effect synchronizes the component with a timer (an external system). It starts the interval after mount and clears it on unmount.

commonMistakes: Using an effect to compute derived state that could be calculated during render.
Using an effect to respond to a click instead of an event handler.
Forgetting that the effect runs after the DOM is painted, not during render.

exercises.prompt: Write an effect that sets document.title to `Count: {count}` whenever count changes.

solution.explanation: document.title is an external (browser) system. The effect re-syncs it whenever count changes.

exercises.prompt: Should you use an effect to compute fullName from firstName and lastName?

solution.explanation: No. That is derived data - just compute const fullName = firstName + ' ' + lastName during render. Effects are for external synchronization, not in-render calculations.

checkYourself: What is an effect for, and what is it NOT for?
When does the setup function run relative to the DOM update?

concepts.title: The dependency array
concepts.summary: The second argument to useEffect lists the reactive values the effect depends on. React re-runs the effect when any listed value changes. [] means run once; omitting it means run after every render.
concepts.explanation: The dependency array controls WHEN the effect re-runs. Include every reactive value (props, state, or values derived from them) that the effect reads. React compares dependencies between renders with Object.is; if any changed, it runs cleanup (if present) then setup again. Three cases: pass [a, b] to run when a or b change; pass [] to run only on mount; omit the array to run after every render (rarely what you want). The linter (exhaustive-deps) helps you list dependencies correctly - do not silence it by lying about deps.
concepts.analogy: Dependencies are like a 'notify me when these specific things change' subscription. List exactly what the effect cares about, no more, no less.
keyPoints: List every reactive value the effect reads.
[a, b]: re-run when a or b change.
[]: run only on mount (and cleanup on unmount).
No array: run after every render.
Trust the exhaustive-deps lint rule; fix the cause, do not omit deps.

code.explanation: The effect reads query, so query is a dependency. Changing it re-runs the fetch; the cleanup ignores stale responses.

commonMistakes: Leaving out a dependency the effect reads, causing stale values.
Passing [] but reading changing props/state inside (a stale closure bug).
Adding objects/functions recreated every render as deps, causing infinite re-runs.

exercises.prompt: This effect always fetches page 1 even when page changes. Fix the dependencies.

solution.explanation: page is read inside but missing from deps, so the effect never re-runs. Listing page makes it re-fetch when page changes.

checkYourself: What goes into the dependency array?
What is the difference between [] and omitting the array?

concepts.title: Cleanup functions and effect timing
concepts.summary: An effect can return a cleanup function. React runs it before re-running the effect and when the component unmounts, so you can undo subscriptions, timers, and connections.
concepts.explanation: If your setup creates something ongoing (an interval, an event listener, a socket, a subscription), you must tear it down to avoid leaks and duplicates. Return a function from the effect; React calls it (1) right before running the effect again due to a dependency change, and (2) when the component unmounts. The pattern is symmetric: setup connects, cleanup disconnects. In development under StrictMode, React deliberately runs setup -> cleanup -> setup once extra to verify your cleanup is correct - if that breaks things, the cleanup is incomplete.
concepts.analogy: Cleanup is like turning off the tap you opened. Every time you are about to re-open it (or you leave the house), you first make sure the previous flow is shut off.
keyPoints: Return a cleanup function from the effect.
Runs before the next effect run and on unmount.
Undo whatever setup created (listeners, timers, subscriptions).
StrictMode runs setup/cleanup an extra time in dev to test correctness.

code.explanation: Setup adds a listener; cleanup removes it on unmount. Without cleanup, listeners would accumulate and leak.

diagram.caption: Cleanup runs before each re-run of the effect and once more on unmount.

commonMistakes: Forgetting cleanup, leaking listeners/timers and stacking duplicates.
Removing a different function instance than the one added (must be the same reference).
Assuming the double setup/cleanup in dev is a bug rather than StrictMode's check.

exercises.prompt: This effect creates a new interval on every render and never clears the old ones. Fix it.

solution.explanation: Add a cleanup to clear the interval and a [] so it sets up once. Otherwise intervals pile up on every render.

checkYourself: When does the cleanup function run?
Why is cleanup important for subscriptions and timers?

concepts.title: Pitfalls: extra or missing dependencies, infinite loops
concepts.summary: Most effect bugs come from wrong dependencies. Missing deps cause stale values; objects/functions recreated each render as deps cause infinite loops; setting state unconditionally inside an effect can loop forever.
concepts.explanation: Two opposite failure modes. (1) Missing dependencies: the effect reads a value not in deps, so it keeps using an old snapshot (stale closure). Fix by listing it. (2) Unstable dependencies: an object, array, or function created during render has a new identity each render; if it is a dependency, the effect re-runs every render. Fix by moving it outside, memoizing with useMemo/useCallback, or restructuring. (3) Infinite loops: an effect that sets state which changes a dependency which re-runs the effect which sets state... Break the cycle with a guard, correct deps, or by computing the value during render instead of in an effect.
concepts.analogy: It is like a thermostat wired to itself: every adjustment triggers another adjustment. You need to break the feedback loop or stop reacting to your own output.
keyPoints: Missing deps -> stale values; list everything the effect reads.
Object/array/function deps recreated each render -> effect re-runs constantly.
Effect sets state that re-triggers itself -> infinite loop.
Fixes: correct deps, useMemo/useCallback, guards, or compute during render.
Often the real fix is: you did not need an effect (next concept).

lines: // Infinite loop: options is a new object every render
const options = { id }; // new identity each render
// Fix: depend on the primitive, build options inside
code.explanation: options changes identity each render, so the effect never settles. Depending on the primitive id and building the object inside fixes the loop.

commonMistakes: Silencing the lint rule instead of fixing the dependency.
Putting freshly-created objects/functions in the dependency array.
Setting state in an effect that depends on that same state without a guard.

exercises.prompt: This effect runs on every render because of the callback dependency. Stabilize it.
starter: // onLoad is a new function each parent render
exercises.hint: Either wrap onLoad in useCallback in the parent, or rethink whether the effect is needed.

lines: // Now <List onLoad={onLoad} /> passes a stable function,
// so the child effect runs only when it truly changes.
solution.explanation: The unstable function identity drives the loop. Memoizing it in the parent gives a stable dependency.

checkYourself: What causes an effect to re-run every single render?
How can an effect create an infinite loop?

concepts.title: When you do NOT need an effect (derived state, event handling)
concepts.summary: Many effects are unnecessary. If you can compute a value during render, do not store it in state and sync with an effect. If something happens in response to a user action, do it in the event handler, not an effect.
concepts.explanation: Two big anti-patterns. (1) Derived state: do not keep a piece of state that is fully computable from other state/props and then use an effect to keep it in sync. Just compute it during render (const fullName = first + ' ' + last). This removes a whole class of bugs and extra renders. (2) Event-driven logic: if code should run because the user clicked or submitted, put it in the handler, not in an effect that watches state. Effects are for synchronizing with external systems, not for reacting to interactions. Legitimate effect uses: subscriptions, fetching tied to props (with care), timers, and integrating non-React code.
concepts.analogy: Storing derived state is like writing your age in a notebook and updating it with an alarm clock - instead, just compute it from your birth date whenever you need it.
keyPoints: Computable from props/state? Compute during render; no effect, no extra state.
Reacting to a click/submit? Do it in the event handler.
Effects are for external synchronization, not interaction logic.
Fewer effects -> fewer bugs and fewer renders.
Need to cache an expensive calc? useMemo, not an effect.

lines: // Anti-pattern: derived state synced with an effect
const count = items.length; // derived during render
code.explanation: count is fully derivable from items, so storing it in state and syncing with an effect is wasteful and bug-prone. Compute it inline.

commonMistakes: Mirroring props into state and syncing with effects.
Running 'on submit' logic in an effect watching a flag instead of in the submit handler.
Using an effect for an expensive calculation instead of useMemo.

exercises.prompt: Remove the unnecessary effect; compute the filtered list during render.

solution.explanation: visible is derived from items, so just compute it during render. No state, no effect, no extra render.

exercises.prompt: A button should POST data when clicked. Effect or event handler?

solution.explanation: Event handler. The POST is a direct response to a user action, so it belongs in onClick - not in an effect watching some state.

checkYourself: How do you handle a value that is computable from other state?
Where should user-action logic live?

concepts.title: useRef: referencing a DOM node
concepts.summary: useRef plus the ref attribute gives you direct access to a DOM element. After render, ref.current points to the node, so you can focus it, measure it, or call its imperative methods.
concepts.explanation: Sometimes you need the actual DOM node - to focus an input, scroll an element, play a video, or measure size. Create a ref with const inputRef = useRef(null), attach it with <input ref={inputRef} />, and after the element mounts, inputRef.current is the DOM node. You typically use it inside event handlers or effects (not during render, since the node does not exist yet on the first render pass). Use refs as an escape hatch for imperative DOM work that React's declarative model does not cover - reach for state first.
concepts.analogy: A ref is like keeping a direct phone number to one specific element, so you can call it and ask it to do something (focus, scroll) without going through React's usual channels.
keyPoints: const ref = useRef(null); then <el ref={ref} />.
After mount, ref.current is the DOM node.
Use it in handlers/effects, not during render.
Good for focus, scroll, measurements, media controls.
Escape hatch: prefer state/props for normal UI logic.

code.explanation: inputRef.current is the real <input>. Clicking the button focuses it directly - something the declarative model alone does not express.

commonMistakes: Reading ref.current during render on first mount (it is still null).
Using a ref to do work React state should handle.
Forgetting to initialize useRef(null) and attach the ref attribute.

exercises.prompt: Create a textarea and a button that scrolls the textarea into view using a ref.

solution.explanation: The ref exposes the DOM node so you can call scrollIntoView on it directly.

checkYourself: When is ref.current populated with the DOM node?
What kinds of tasks are refs good for?

concepts.title: useRef: storing a mutable value without re-rendering
concepts.summary: A ref is also a plain mutable container that survives re-renders. Changing ref.current does NOT trigger a render, making it ideal for values you want to remember but not display.
concepts.explanation: Beyond DOM nodes, useRef gives you a box { current: value } that persists across renders and whose mutation does not cause a re-render. This contrasts with state: changing state re-renders; changing a ref does not. Use refs for things like a timer/interval id, the previous value of a prop, a flag that the effect should ignore the next run, or any instance-like value that the UI does not need to reflect. Rule of thumb: if changing the value should update what the user sees, use state; if it is just bookkeeping, use a ref.
concepts.analogy: A ref is like a sticky note on your monitor: you can scribble and update it anytime, and it does not redraw the whole screen. State is information printed into the UI itself.
keyPoints: useRef returns a persistent { current } box.
Mutating ref.current does not re-render.
Great for timer ids, previous values, instance flags.
State re-renders; refs do not.
If the UI must reflect the change, use state instead.

lines: const intervalRef = useRef(null); // bookkeeping, no re-render
code.explanation: time is state (shown in UI). The interval id is bookkeeping stored in a ref, so updating it does not trigger renders.

commonMistakes: Storing UI-visible data in a ref and wondering why the screen does not update.
Expecting a ref change to re-render the component.
Reading/writing ref.current during render in a way that breaks purity.

exercises.prompt: Track how many times a component rendered without causing extra renders.

lines: // renders.current holds the count; updating it does not re-render
solution.explanation: A ref accumulates the count across renders without triggering more renders, unlike state.

exercises.prompt: You need to show a 'likes' number that updates on screen. Ref or state?

solution.explanation: State - the value is displayed and must trigger a re-render when it changes. Refs do not re-render, so the UI would not update.

checkYourself: Does changing ref.current cause a re-render?
When do you choose a ref over state?

concepts.title: Working with the DOM via a ref and managing focus
concepts.summary: Use refs for imperative DOM tasks React does not handle declaratively: focusing inputs, scrolling, measuring, selecting text, and integrating non-React widgets. Often the work goes in an effect after mount.
concepts.explanation: Common imperative needs: focus an input when a modal opens, scroll a chat to the bottom after new messages, measure an element's size, or hand a DOM node to a third-party library (a chart, a map). You attach a ref, then perform the call in an event handler or in an effect (so the node exists). For focus on mount, an effect with [] is typical. Keep this minimal - if React can do it declaratively, prefer that. Refs are the controlled escape hatch when it cannot.
concepts.analogy: It is like reaching past the autopilot to flip one specific switch by hand. You use it sparingly, for the few things the autopilot (declarative React) does not control.
keyPoints: Typical tasks: focus, scroll, measure, select, integrate third-party widgets.
Do DOM work in handlers or effects (node must exist).
Focus-on-mount: effect with [].
Use refs as a last resort, not the default tool.
Read measurements after layout (consider useLayoutEffect for sync reads).

lines: dialogRef.current.focus(); // focus when the modal mounts
code.explanation: On mount, the effect focuses the dialog so keyboard users land inside it - a classic imperative-via-ref task.

commonMistakes: Trying to focus/measure during render before the node exists.
Overusing refs for things state could express.
Forgetting accessibility (tabIndex, roles) when managing focus manually.

exercises.prompt: Auto-focus a search input when the component first appears.

solution.explanation: The [] effect runs once after mount, when the input exists, and focuses it.

checkYourself: Why perform focus in an effect rather than during render?
Name three legitimate imperative tasks for a ref.

concepts.title: forwardRef (and ref-as-prop in React 19)
concepts.summary: To let a parent attach a ref to a DOM node inside your custom component, older React uses forwardRef. In React 19 you can accept ref as a normal prop, and forwardRef is no longer required.
concepts.explanation: By default, ref does not pass through a custom component like a normal prop - if you write <MyInput ref={r} />, React does not know which inner DOM node you mean. The classic solution is forwardRef: wrap your component so it receives (props, ref) and forwards ref to the desired element. As of React 19, ref is available as a regular prop, so a function component can simply read props.ref and attach it - making forwardRef unnecessary for new code. You will still see forwardRef widely in existing codebases.
concepts.analogy: forwardRef is like a receptionist passing your call through to the right extension. In React 19, the extension is dialable directly - no receptionist needed.
keyPoints: ref is not forwarded through custom components automatically (pre-19).
forwardRef((props, ref) => ...) forwards a ref to an inner node.
React 19: accept ref as a normal prop; forwardRef is not needed.
Legacy codebases still use forwardRef extensively.
Useful for design-system inputs/buttons that expose their DOM node.

lines: // React 19: ref as a normal prop
code.explanation: Both expose the inner <input> to the parent's ref. React 19 drops the forwardRef wrapper and reads ref straight from props.

commonMistakes: Expecting <Custom ref={r} /> to work without forwardRef in pre-19 React.
Forwarding the ref to the wrong inner element.
Adding forwardRef in new React 19 code where a ref prop suffices.

exercises.prompt: Write a reusable TextBox (pre-19) that forwards a ref to its inner input.

solution.explanation: forwardRef passes the parent's ref down to the actual <input> so the parent can focus or measure it.

checkYourself: Why does a ref not pass through a custom component by default (pre-19)?
What changed about refs in React 19?

concepts.title: Lifting state up
concepts.summary: When two components need to share or stay in sync with the same data, move that state up to their closest common parent. The parent owns the state and passes it down with callbacks to update it.
concepts.explanation: If sibling components each kept their own copy of a value, they would drift apart. The fix is to lift the state to the nearest ancestor that contains both, then pass the value down as a prop and pass down a setter/callback so children can request changes. Now there is a single source of truth. This is the most common pattern for component communication: data flows down, events flow up via callbacks. It keeps behavior predictable and avoids duplicate, out-of-sync state.
concepts.analogy: Two roommates arguing over the thermostat each set their own dial and conflict. Lifting state up is putting one shared thermostat in the hallway that both can request changes to.
keyPoints: Shared/synced data belongs in the closest common parent.
Parent passes the value down as a prop.
Parent passes a callback down so children can update it.
Single source of truth; data down, events up.
Avoids duplicated, diverging state in siblings.

code.explanation: text lives in Parent. Input updates it via the onChange callback; Preview reads it. Both siblings stay in sync through one source of truth.

diagram.caption: State lives in the common parent: data flows down, change requests flow back up.

commonMistakes: Keeping separate state in siblings that should be shared.
Lifting state higher than necessary (hurts performance and clarity).
Forgetting to pass the callback so children cannot update the shared value.

exercises.prompt: Two buttons should both reflect and toggle the same 'on/off' state. Sketch where the state goes.

solution.explanation: State lives in the common parent Panel; both Switches read on and call onToggle, staying in sync.

checkYourself: Where should shared state live?
How do children request changes to lifted state?

concepts.title: useContext and Context: createContext, Provider, consuming
concepts.summary: Context lets you share a value with a whole subtree without passing it through every intermediate component. Create it with createContext, provide it with a Provider, and read it with useContext.
concepts.explanation: Some data (theme, current user, language) is needed by many components at different depths. Passing it through every level as props is tedious. Context solves this: createContext(defaultValue) makes a context object; wrap part of the tree in <MyContext.Provider value={...}>; any descendant calls useContext(MyContext) to read the current value - no prop threading. When the Provider's value changes, all consumers re-render. Context is for relatively stable, widely-needed data, not as a general replacement for all props or for high-frequency updates.
concepts.analogy: Context is like the building's central radio broadcast: instead of hand-delivering a memo to every floor, you broadcast once and any room can tune in.
keyPoints: createContext(default) creates the context.
<Context.Provider value={...}> supplies the value to a subtree.
useContext(Context) reads the nearest Provider's value.
Changing the Provider value re-renders all consumers.
Best for stable, broadly-needed data (theme, user, locale).

lines: function Toolbar() { return <Button />; } // no theme prop needed
code.explanation: Button reads the theme directly via useContext, even though Toolbar never passed it. The Provider broadcasts the value to the subtree.

diagram.caption: The Provider supplies a value; deep consumers read it directly, skipping intermediate props.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to wrap consumers in a Provider (they get the default value).
Putting a new object/array as value inline, re-rendering all consumers each render.
Using Context for fast-changing values, causing wide re-renders.

exercises.prompt: Create a UserContext providing { name: 'Illia' } and read name in a deep component.

solution.explanation: Profile reads the provided object via useContext without any props threaded through ancestors.

checkYourself: What three pieces make Context work?
What happens to consumers when the Provider value changes?

concepts.title: Context vs prop drilling
concepts.summary: Prop drilling is passing a prop through many intermediate components that do not use it, just to reach a deep one. Context removes that threading, but it is not a free replacement for all props.
concepts.explanation: Prop drilling becomes painful when a value travels through several layers that only forward it. Context fixes the worst cases (theme, auth, locale) by letting deep components read the value directly. But Context has trade-offs: it couples consumers to a provider, can cause broad re-renders if the value changes often, and can make data flow less explicit. Guidance: prefer plain props for local, few-level passing; use composition (passing JSX as children) to avoid some drilling; reach for Context when many components across the tree need the same fairly-stable value.
concepts.analogy: Prop drilling is passing a note person-to-person down a long line. Context is a PA announcement. Use the announcement for things everyone needs, not for a quiet word meant for the person next to you.
keyPoints: Prop drilling: threading a prop through non-consuming layers.
Context removes threading for widely-needed values.
Context costs: coupling, potential broad re-renders, less explicit flow.
Prefer props for shallow passing; composition can avoid drilling.
Use Context for stable, cross-tree data.

lines: // Composition can remove drilling without Context:
// Pass the finished element down instead of the raw prop
code.explanation: By passing JSX (sidebar) rather than the raw user prop, Layout does not need to forward user at all - one way to dodge drilling without Context.

commonMistakes: Reaching for Context for a value passed through just one or two levels.
Ignoring composition, which often removes drilling more simply.
Putting volatile data in Context and triggering wide re-renders.

exercises.prompt: A value is passed through 5 layers but used by 20 deep components across the tree. Props or Context?

solution.explanation: Context fits: many components across the tree need the same value, and threading it through every layer is wasteful. For one or two layers, plain props are simpler.

checkYourself: What is prop drilling?
When is composition a better fix than Context?

concepts.title: useReducer: reducer, dispatch, and when it beats useState
concepts.summary: useReducer manages state with a reducer function: given the current state and an action, it returns the next state. You trigger changes with dispatch(action). It shines when state logic is complex or many actions update related state.
concepts.explanation: useReducer(reducer, initialState) returns [state, dispatch]. The reducer is a pure function (state, action) => newState that centralizes all transitions in one place. Instead of scattering many setState calls, components dispatch descriptive actions like { type: 'add', payload }, and the reducer decides how state changes. Prefer useReducer over useState when: transitions are complex, the next state depends intricately on the previous, multiple sub-values change together, or you want to test/centralize the logic. For simple independent values, useState is lighter.
concepts.analogy: A reducer is like a vending machine: you press a labeled button (dispatch an action), and the machine's fixed internal logic decides what comes out (the next state). All the rules live inside the machine.
keyPoints: useReducer(reducer, init) -> [state, dispatch].
reducer(state, action) is pure and returns the next state.
dispatch(action) triggers a transition.
Centralizes complex/related state logic.
Prefer over useState for intricate transitions; useState for simple values.

code.explanation: All transitions live in reducer. Components only dispatch intent ('inc', 'dec'); the reducer computes the next state purely.

diagram.caption: dispatch sends an action to the reducer, which returns the next state, re-rendering the component.

commonMistakes: Mutating state inside the reducer instead of returning a new object.
Putting side effects in the reducer (it must be pure).
Using useReducer for trivial single values where useState is simpler.

exercises.prompt: Write a reducer for a todo list supporting 'add' and 'remove' actions (state is an array).

solution.explanation: Each case returns a new array (immutability). Components dispatch { type: 'add', id, text } or { type: 'remove', id }.

exercises.prompt: You have one boolean toggle. useState or useReducer?

solution.explanation: useState. A single independent boolean does not need a reducer; reach for useReducer when transitions get complex or many related values change together.

checkYourself: What is the signature of a reducer?
When does useReducer beat useState?

concepts.title: Controlled vs uncontrolled components
concepts.summary: A controlled component's value is driven by React state (value + onChange). An uncontrolled component keeps its own value in the DOM, which you read via a ref when needed (often with defaultValue).
concepts.explanation: Two ways to handle form inputs. Controlled: React state is the source of truth; the input always shows state, and onChange keeps state updated. This gives full control - validation, formatting, conditional disabling - at the cost of wiring every field. Uncontrolled: the DOM holds the value; you set an initial defaultValue and read the final value from a ref on submit. This is simpler for basic forms and integrates with non-React code, but you cannot easily react to each keystroke. Choose controlled when you need to respond to or constrain input; uncontrolled for simple, fire-on-submit forms.
concepts.analogy: Controlled is driving with your hands on the wheel the whole time. Uncontrolled is letting the car coast and only checking the odometer at the end of the trip.
keyPoints: Controlled: value={state} + onChange; React owns the value.
Uncontrolled: DOM owns the value; use defaultValue and read via a ref.
Controlled enables live validation/formatting/disabling.
Uncontrolled is simpler and good for submit-only forms.
Do not mix value and defaultValue on the same input.

code.explanation: Controlled mirrors every keystroke into state. Uncontrolled lets the DOM hold the value and reads it on demand via the ref.

commonMistakes: Mixing value and defaultValue on one input.
Expecting per-keystroke logic from an uncontrolled input.
Making everything controlled when a simple uncontrolled form would do.

exercises.prompt: You need to disable the submit button until the email contains '@'. Controlled or uncontrolled?

solution.explanation: Controlled. You must read the value on every change to update the button's disabled state - that requires state + onChange.

checkYourself: What owns the value in a controlled vs uncontrolled input?
When is uncontrolled a reasonable choice?

concepts.title: Resetting component state with key
concepts.summary: Changing a component's key prop tells React to treat it as a brand-new instance, discarding its old state. This is the idiomatic way to fully reset a component when an identifying value changes.
concepts.explanation: React identifies a component instance by its position in the tree AND its key. If you give a component a key that changes, React unmounts the old instance and mounts a fresh one - all internal state resets. This is perfect for cases like an edit form that should reset when you switch which item is being edited: key={selectedId}. It is cleaner than manually clearing every field in an effect. Use a key tied to the identity that should drive a reset; do not abuse it for normal updates.
concepts.analogy: Changing the key is like swapping in a new whiteboard instead of erasing the old one. The new board starts blank - all previous scribbles (state) are gone.
keyPoints: React identity = position + key.
Changing key remounts the component (fresh state).
Great for resetting forms when the selected item changes.
Cleaner than manually clearing state in an effect.
Use a key reflecting the identity that should trigger reset.

lines: // Changing userId remounts EditForm, clearing its internal state
const [draft, setDraft] = useState(''); // resets when key changes
code.explanation: When userId changes, the key changes, so React mounts a fresh EditForm and draft resets to '' - no manual clearing needed.

commonMistakes: Clearing many fields by hand in an effect when a key reset is simpler.
Using an unstable key (like an index) that resets unexpectedly.
Overusing key resets where a normal prop update would suffice.

exercises.prompt: You have a CommentBox with local draft state. Make it reset whenever postId changes.

solution.explanation: Tying the key to postId remounts CommentBox on change, discarding the old draft automatically.

checkYourself: How does changing key affect a component's state?
When is a key reset cleaner than manual clearing?

concepts.title: Extracting logic into use... hooks
concepts.summary: A custom hook is a function whose name starts with 'use' that calls other hooks. It packages reusable stateful logic so multiple components can share behavior without sharing state.
concepts.explanation: When several components need the same logic (tracking window size, fetching, a form field, a toggle), you extract it into a custom hook: function useWindowWidth() { ... }. Inside, you call built-in hooks (useState, useEffect, etc.) and return whatever the caller needs. Crucially, custom hooks share LOGIC, not state: each component that calls the hook gets its own independent state. Naming with the 'use' prefix is required so React's tooling and the Rules of Hooks apply. Custom hooks keep components small and make behavior testable and reusable.
concepts.analogy: A custom hook is like a recipe you can reuse in many kitchens. Everyone follows the same steps (logic), but each kitchen makes its own separate dish (state).
keyPoints: A custom hook is a 'use'-prefixed function that calls hooks.
It returns values/handlers the caller needs.
Shares logic, not state - each call has independent state.
Keeps components small and behavior reusable/testable.
The 'use' prefix is mandatory for the rules/tooling to apply.

code.explanation: useWindowWidth packages the resize logic once. Any component can call it and get the current width, each with its own state.

commonMistakes: Expecting two components calling the same hook to share state (they do not).
Naming the function without the 'use' prefix.
Putting too much unrelated logic into one hook.

exercises.prompt: Write a useToggle hook returning [value, toggle] for a boolean.

solution.explanation: It encapsulates the boolean and its toggler; each caller gets independent state.

checkYourself: Do two components sharing a custom hook share state?
Why must custom hooks start with 'use'?

concepts.title: Rules of Hooks: top level only, React functions only
concepts.summary: Two rules make hooks work: (1) only call hooks at the top level of a component or custom hook - never inside conditions, loops, or nested functions; (2) only call hooks from React function components or other custom hooks.
concepts.explanation: React matches each hook call to its state by call ORDER, which must be identical on every render. So you must always call the same hooks, in the same order: no hooks inside if/for/while, no early-return before a hook, no hooks in callbacks or regular functions. Need conditional logic? Put the condition INSIDE the hook (e.g., inside the effect), not around the hook call. Rule two: hooks only belong in React function components or custom hooks - not in class components, event handlers, or plain JS functions. The eslint-plugin-react-hooks rule enforces both and will flag violations.
concepts.analogy: Hooks are like a fixed checklist read top to bottom every render. If you skip or reorder items (conditional hooks), the bookkeeping lines up the wrong answers with the wrong questions.
keyPoints: Call hooks at the top level only - no conditions, loops, or nested functions.
No early return before your hook calls.
Call hooks only from components or custom hooks.
Put conditions inside the hook, not around it.
The hooks lint rule enforces this.
