lines: // WRONG: hook inside a condition (order varies between renders)
const [x, setX] = useState(0); // breaks the rules
// RIGHT: hook at top level; condition inside
code.explanation: Good always calls the same hooks in the same order, moving the condition inside the effect. Bad changes the hook order based on a prop, which corrupts state mapping.

commonMistakes: Calling useState/useEffect inside an if or loop.
Returning early from a component before its hooks run.
Calling hooks in event handlers or plain functions.

exercises.prompt: This violates the Rules of Hooks. Refactor it.

solution.explanation: Move the hook above the early return so hooks always run in the same order. The conditional return comes after all hook calls.

checkYourself: Why must hooks be called in the same order every render?
Where are you allowed to call hooks?

concepts.title: What triggers a re-render (props, state, context)
concepts.summary: A component re-renders when its own state changes, when its parent re-renders, or when a context it consumes changes. Re-rendering means React calls the function again to compute new JSX.
concepts.explanation: There are exactly three triggers. (1) State change: calling a state setter (useState/useReducer) schedules a re-render of that component. (2) Parent re-render: when a parent renders, by default all of its children render too, regardless of whether their props changed. (3) Context change: a component that reads a context via useContext re-renders when that context's value changes. Note what is NOT a trigger: mutating a ref, or props 'looking' different by value alone - what matters is that an ancestor re-rendered or state/context changed. Understanding this is the basis for performance work: most 'unnecessary' renders come from a parent re-rendering and dragging children along.
concepts.analogy: Re-renders cascade like a row of dominoes: knock over the parent and the children fall too, even the ones that did not really need to move.
keyPoints: Three triggers: own state change, parent re-render, consumed context change.
By default, a parent render re-renders ALL its children.
Re-render = React calls the component again, not necessarily a DOM change.
Ref mutations do NOT trigger re-renders.
Most wasted renders come from cascading parent renders.

lines: Self --> Kids[All children re-render by default]
diagram.caption: The three re-render triggers and how a parent render cascades to children.

commonMistakes: Assuming a child only re-renders when its props change (it also renders when the parent does).
Thinking every re-render touches the DOM (often the commit is a no-op).
Expecting ref changes to re-render.

exercises.prompt: A parent re-renders but passes the exact same props to <Child />. Does Child re-render by default?

solution.explanation: Yes. By default children re-render whenever the parent does, regardless of prop values. To skip that, wrap Child in React.memo (next concept) and keep prop identities stable.

exercises.prompt: Name the three things that cause a component to re-render.

solution.explanation: Its own state changing, its parent re-rendering, or a context it consumes changing value.

checkYourself: What are the three re-render triggers?
Does a parent render re-render its children even if props are unchanged?

concepts.title: React.memo
concepts.summary: React.memo wraps a component so it skips re-rendering when its props are unchanged (shallow comparison). It breaks the default 'parent renders -> child renders' cascade for that component.
concepts.explanation: Wrap a component: const Child = React.memo(function Child(props) {...}). Now when the parent re-renders, React compares the new props to the previous ones with a shallow (Object.is) check; if they are equal, it reuses the previous render and skips the child. This is useful for expensive children that receive the same props often. Two caveats: the comparison is shallow, so object/array/function props that are recreated each render will look different and defeat memo (see referential equality); and memo only helps when the parent re-renders frequently with stable props. You can pass a custom comparison function as the second argument for special cases.
concepts.analogy: React.memo is like a bouncer checking IDs at the door: if the props are the same as last time, it says 'you have not changed, no need to redo your work' and lets the old result stand.
keyPoints: React.memo(Component) skips re-render when props are shallowly equal.
Breaks the parent->child render cascade for that component.
Shallow comparison: new object/array/function props defeat it.
Best for expensive components that often get the same props.
Optional second arg: a custom areEqual(prev, next) comparator.

code.explanation: Clicking re-renders Parent, but Expensive's value prop is unchanged, so memo skips it - 'render Expensive' does not log again.

commonMistakes: Passing inline objects/arrays/functions, which change identity each render and defeat memo.
Wrapping everything in memo prematurely (adds comparison cost with no benefit).
Expecting memo to help when props genuinely change every render.

exercises.prompt: Expensive is memoized but still re-renders every time. The parent passes style={{ color: 'red' }}. Why, and how do you fix it?
exercises.hint: A new object literal is created on every render.

solution.explanation: The inline object had a new identity each render, so memo saw 'different props'. Memoizing the object keeps a stable reference.

checkYourself: What comparison does React.memo perform?
Why can object/function props defeat memo?

concepts.title: useMemo for memoizing computations
concepts.summary: useMemo caches the result of an expensive calculation between renders, recomputing only when its dependencies change. It also keeps object/array values referentially stable.
concepts.explanation: useMemo(() => compute(a, b), [a, b]) runs compute on the first render and re-runs it only when a or b change; otherwise it returns the cached value. Two uses: (1) skip an expensive calculation (sorting/filtering a big list, heavy math) on renders where inputs did not change; (2) preserve a stable reference for an object or array you pass to a memoized child or use as an effect dependency. Do not overuse it - for cheap calculations the bookkeeping costs more than it saves. Measure first; reach for useMemo when a computation is genuinely heavy or when referential stability is needed.
concepts.analogy: useMemo is like saving the result of a slow calculation in a labeled drawer. As long as the inputs on the label match, you grab the saved answer instead of recomputing.
keyPoints: useMemo(fn, deps) caches fn's result; recomputes only when deps change.
Use for expensive computations or to keep a stable object/array reference.
Returns the cached value, not a function (that is useCallback).
Do not memoize cheap work - it can be net-negative.
Dependencies follow the same rules as useEffect.

lines: return products.filter((p) => p.name.includes(query)); // heavy on big lists
code.explanation: The filter re-runs only when products or query change. On unrelated re-renders, the cached visible array is reused.

commonMistakes: Wrapping trivial calculations in useMemo (overhead with no gain).
Listing wrong dependencies, so the cached value goes stale.
Confusing useMemo (caches a value) with useCallback (caches a function).

exercises.prompt: Memoize a sorted copy of an array `rows` by a `sortKey`, recomputing only when either changes.

solution.explanation: We copy before sorting (sort mutates), and cache the result keyed on rows and sortKey.

exercises.prompt: Does useMemo return a function or a value?

solution.explanation: A value (the result of calling the factory). To memoize a function's identity, use useCallback.

checkYourself: What two problems does useMemo solve?
When is using useMemo a net negative?

concepts.title: useCallback for memoizing function identity
concepts.summary: useCallback returns a memoized version of a function that keeps the same identity between renders until its dependencies change. It is useMemo specialized for functions.
concepts.explanation: Every render creates new function instances. If you pass a handler to a memoized child or use it as an effect dependency, that fresh identity defeats the optimization or re-triggers the effect. useCallback(fn, deps) returns the same function reference across renders, creating a new one only when deps change. It is exactly useMemo(() => fn, deps). Use it when a function is passed to a React.memo child, used in a dependency array, or otherwise needs a stable identity. Like useMemo, do not sprinkle it everywhere - it only pays off when identity stability actually matters.
concepts.analogy: useCallback is like keeping the same phone number instead of getting a new one every day. People who saved your number (memoized children, effects) are not disturbed unless the number truly changes.
keyPoints: useCallback(fn, deps) keeps a stable function identity until deps change.
Equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, deps).
Use when passing handlers to memoized children or as effect deps.
Pointless if the function is not passed down or depended upon.
Stale closures: include in deps anything the function reads.

code.explanation: Without useCallback, onAction would be a new function each render and Child (memoized) would re-render anyway. The stable callback lets memo do its job.

commonMistakes: Using useCallback for a function that is never passed to a memoized child or effect.
Omitting dependencies the function reads, capturing stale values.
Expecting useCallback alone to help when the child is not memoized.

exercises.prompt: A memoized Child receives onClick={() => doThing()} written inline in the parent. Does memo prevent Child's re-render?

solution.explanation: No. The inline arrow is a new function every render, so Child sees a changed prop and re-renders. Wrap it with useCallback to stabilize the identity.

checkYourself: How does useCallback relate to useMemo?
When does useCallback actually help performance?

concepts.title: Referential equality and why it breaks memoization
concepts.summary: React compares props and dependencies by reference (Object.is) for non-primitives. Objects, arrays, and functions created during render are new each time, so they look 'different' even when their contents match - silently defeating memo, useMemo, and effects.
concepts.explanation: Primitives (numbers, strings, booleans) compare by value, so 5 === 5. But {a:1} !== {a:1} and () => {} !== () => {} - two literals with identical contents are different references. React's shallow comparisons (in React.memo, useMemo/useCallback deps, useEffect deps) use reference equality for these. So passing an inline object/array/function recreates it every render, making memoization think the input changed. The fix is to stabilize identities: memoize objects/arrays with useMemo, functions with useCallback, hoist constants out of the component, or restructure so you pass primitives. This single idea explains most 'why is my memo not working' bugs.
concepts.analogy: Two identical-looking houses still have different addresses. React checks the address (reference), not whether the houses look the same (contents). Build the house once and keep its address stable.
keyPoints: Primitives compare by value; objects/arrays/functions by reference.
Inline literals get a NEW reference every render.
Shallow comparisons (memo, deps) see new references as 'changed'.
Stabilize with useMemo (objects/arrays), useCallback (functions), or hoisting.
Root cause of most failed memoizations and looping effects.

lines: const config = { debug: true };          // new object each render
const handle = () => console.log('hi');  // new function each render
code.explanation: In Bad, the object and function are recreated each render, so a memoized Child re-renders anyway. Good keeps both references stable.

commonMistakes: Comparing objects/arrays expecting value equality.
Passing inline {}/[]/() => {} to memoized children.
Putting freshly-created values in dependency arrays, causing loops.

exercises.prompt: What does ({} === {}) evaluate to, and what about (3 === 3)?

solution.explanation: {} === {} is false (two different references). 3 === 3 is true (primitives compare by value). That difference is why object props break shallow memo checks.

exercises.prompt: An effect with deps [options] runs every render. options = { sort: 'asc' } is defined in the component body. Fix it.

solution.explanation: options had a new reference each render, so the effect always saw a 'changed' dependency. Memoizing stabilizes it.

checkYourself: Why does {a:1} !== {a:1}?
How do you stabilize an object, array, or function across renders?

concepts.title: useId: stable unique ids (SSR-safe)
concepts.summary: useId generates a unique, stable string id that matches between server and client rendering. Use it to link form elements (label/input, aria attributes), not as a key for list items.
concepts.explanation: When you need an id to connect accessibility-related elements - htmlFor on a label pointing to an input's id, or aria-describedby - hardcoding ids breaks if the component renders multiple times, and generating random ids breaks server-side rendering because the server and client would produce different strings (a hydration mismatch). useId solves this: it returns a deterministic unique id that is identical on server and client. Call it once and derive multiple related ids from it (id + '-error'). It is NOT for list keys (use your data id there) and NOT for CSS selectors.
concepts.analogy: useId is like a guaranteed-unique seat number printed identically on both your online ticket and the one at the gate, so they always match.
keyPoints: Generates a unique, stable id consistent across SSR and client.
Use for accessibility links: label/input, aria-* references.
Derive related ids from one call: `${id}-desc`.
Not for list keys; not for CSS class/selector names.
Avoids hydration mismatches that random ids would cause.

code.explanation: One useId call generates a base id reused to link the label, input, and hint - stable across server and client.

commonMistakes: Using useId as a list item key (use the data's id instead).
Generating ids with Math.random(), breaking SSR hydration.
Calling useId in a loop expecting many ids (call once, derive suffixes).

exercises.prompt: Link a checkbox and its label accessibly using useId.

solution.explanation: htmlFor matches the input id, and useId keeps it unique and SSR-safe.

checkYourself: Why not use Math.random() for an input id in an SSR app?
What is useId NOT meant for?

concepts.title: useLayoutEffect vs useEffect: the timing difference
concepts.summary: useLayoutEffect runs synchronously after DOM mutations but BEFORE the browser paints; useEffect runs after paint. Use useLayoutEffect only when you must measure or adjust the DOM before the user sees it.
concepts.explanation: Both hooks run after the commit, but at different moments. useEffect fires asynchronously after the browser has painted the new frame - good for the vast majority of side effects (fetching, subscriptions, logging). useLayoutEffect fires synchronously after React updates the DOM but before paint, so any DOM reads/writes you do there happen before the user sees the frame, preventing visual flicker. The trade-off: useLayoutEffect blocks painting, so overusing it hurts performance. Reach for it only for layout measurements (reading size/position) and immediate DOM adjustments (e.g., positioning a tooltip) where a flash of the wrong layout would be visible.
concepts.analogy: useLayoutEffect is like adjusting the stage set after the lights are wired but before the curtain rises - the audience never sees the tweak. useEffect adjusts after the curtain is already up.
keyPoints: useEffect: runs after paint (async); default choice.
useLayoutEffect: runs after DOM update, before paint (sync).
Use useLayoutEffect to measure layout / prevent visible flicker.
It blocks painting - overuse hurts performance.
Same signature and dependency rules as useEffect.

code.explanation: We measure the element and position it before paint, so the user never sees it jump from a wrong position to the right one.

commonMistakes: Using useLayoutEffect everywhere, blocking paint unnecessarily.
Doing data fetching in useLayoutEffect (belongs in useEffect).
Forgetting it can cause SSR warnings (no layout on the server).

exercises.prompt: You read an element's width and reposition it to avoid a visible flash. Which hook?

solution.explanation: useLayoutEffect - it runs before paint, so the measurement and reposition happen before the user sees the frame. useEffect would let the wrong position flash first.

checkYourself: What is the timing difference between the two hooks?
Why should useLayoutEffect be used sparingly?

concepts.title: useImperativeHandle: customizing what a ref exposes
concepts.summary: useImperativeHandle lets a component control what its parent's ref can access, exposing a custom set of methods instead of the raw DOM node. It is used with forwardRef (or a ref prop in React 19).
concepts.explanation: Normally a forwarded ref points at a DOM node, giving the parent full access. Sometimes you want to expose only a curated API - e.g., a custom input that offers focus() and clear() but hides everything else. useImperativeHandle(ref, () => ({ focus, clear }), deps) defines exactly what ref.current becomes for the parent. This keeps the component's internals encapsulated while still allowing controlled imperative calls. Use it rarely; most needs are better met declaratively with props. It is mainly for reusable component libraries that must offer imperative escape hatches.
concepts.analogy: useImperativeHandle is like a TV remote: the parent gets a few labeled buttons (focus, clear) instead of being handed the TV's open circuit board (the raw DOM node).
keyPoints: Defines the custom object a parent's ref receives.
Pairs with forwardRef (or a ref prop in React 19).
Exposes a curated method API, hiding internals.
Use sparingly; prefer declarative props.
Common in component libraries needing imperative methods.

lines: // Parent: const r = useRef(null); r.current.focus(); r.current.clear();
code.explanation: The parent's ref exposes only focus and clear, not the raw input node. Internals stay encapsulated.

commonMistakes: Using it where props would do the job declaratively.
Forgetting the dependency array, recreating the handle every render.
Exposing too much, leaking internals you meant to hide.

exercises.prompt: Expose a single scrollToTop() method from a List component via a ref.

solution.explanation: The parent ref gets just scrollToTop; the inner scrollable div stays private.

checkYourself: What does useImperativeHandle control?
Why prefer props over imperative handles when possible?

concepts.title: useDebugValue: labeling custom hooks in DevTools
concepts.summary: useDebugValue adds a readable label to a custom hook so it shows useful information in React DevTools. It is a development aid only and has no effect on behavior.
concepts.explanation: When you build custom hooks, React DevTools lists them but cannot know what internal value matters. useDebugValue(value) attaches a label that appears next to the hook in DevTools, making debugging easier - for example showing whether a useOnlineStatus hook is 'Online' or 'Offline'. You can pass a formatting function as a second argument so the (possibly expensive) formatting runs only when DevTools is open. It does nothing in production and should be used only in reusable hooks where the label genuinely aids inspection; it is unnecessary for simple hooks.
concepts.analogy: useDebugValue is like a name tag you pin on an otherwise anonymous helper so that when you inspect the team (DevTools), you can tell who is who at a glance.
keyPoints: Adds a label to a custom hook in React DevTools.
Development/debugging only; no runtime behavior change.
Optional formatter runs lazily (only when inspected).
Use in shared hooks where a label helps; skip for trivial ones.
Only meaningful inside custom hooks.

code.explanation: In DevTools, useOnlineStatus displays 'Online' or 'Offline' next to it, making the hook's current state obvious.

commonMistakes: Adding it to trivial hooks where it just adds noise.
Doing expensive formatting inline instead of via the formatter argument.
Expecting it to affect production behavior (it does not).

exercises.prompt: Does useDebugValue change what your hook returns or how it behaves at runtime?

solution.explanation: No. It only adds a label visible in React DevTools and has no effect on the hook's return value or runtime behavior, and nothing in production.

checkYourself: What is useDebugValue for?
Why pass a formatter function as the second argument?

concepts.title: Error boundaries: getDerivedStateFromError and componentDidCatch
concepts.summary: An error boundary is a class component that catches JavaScript errors thrown during rendering in its child tree, shows a fallback UI, and prevents the whole app from crashing. It uses getDerivedStateFromError and/or componentDidCatch.
concepts.explanation: If a component throws during render, React unmounts the whole tree by default - a blank screen. Error boundaries contain the damage: a class component that implements static getDerivedStateFromError(error) (to update state and render a fallback) and/or componentDidCatch(error, info) (to log the error) will catch errors from its descendants and display a fallback instead. There is no Hook equivalent yet, so boundaries are still classes (or you use a library like react-error-boundary). They catch render, lifecycle, and constructor errors of children - but NOT errors in event handlers, async code, or the boundary itself; use try/catch for those.
concepts.analogy: An error boundary is like a circuit breaker: when one part shorts out, it trips locally and keeps the rest of the house powered, instead of burning everything down.
keyPoints: Catches render-time errors in the child tree; shows a fallback.
getDerivedStateFromError: set state to render the fallback.
componentDidCatch: log the error/details.
Still class-based (no built-in Hook version yet).
Does NOT catch errors in event handlers, async code, or itself.

code.explanation: If Risky throws during render, getDerivedStateFromError flips hasError and the boundary renders a fallback instead of crashing the app.

diagram.caption: A render error in a child is caught by the boundary, which swaps in a fallback.

commonMistakes: Expecting a boundary to catch errors in event handlers or async code (use try/catch).
Putting one boundary at the very top only (granular boundaries isolate failures better).
Forgetting boundaries cannot catch their own render errors.

exercises.prompt: Will an error boundary catch an error thrown inside an onClick handler?

solution.explanation: No. Error boundaries catch errors during rendering, lifecycle, and constructors of children - not in event handlers or async callbacks. Wrap those in try/catch.

exercises.prompt: Which method do you implement to RENDER a fallback, and which to LOG the error?

solution.explanation: getDerivedStateFromError to update state and render the fallback; componentDidCatch to log the error and its component stack.

checkYourself: What kinds of errors do boundaries NOT catch?
What are the roles of the two methods?

concepts.title: Portals with createPortal (modals, tooltips outside the parent tree)
concepts.summary: createPortal renders children into a different DOM node (outside the parent's DOM hierarchy) while keeping them in the same React tree. Ideal for modals, tooltips, and dropdowns that must escape overflow/z-index constraints.
concepts.explanation: Sometimes a component's DOM needs to live elsewhere - a modal should attach to document.body to avoid being clipped by a parent's overflow:hidden or trapped under a low z-index. createPortal(children, domNode) renders children into domNode in the real DOM, yet React still treats them as children of the component that created the portal. That means context, state, and event bubbling work as if they were nested normally - a click inside a portal even bubbles to ancestors in the React tree, not the DOM tree. So you get DOM placement freedom without losing React's data flow.
concepts.analogy: A portal is like a video feed: the camera (component) stays in its room in the React tree, but the picture is displayed on a screen in another room (a different DOM node).
keyPoints: createPortal(children, container) renders into a different DOM node.
Children remain in the same React tree (context, events still flow).
Events bubble along the React tree, not the DOM tree.
Great for modals, tooltips, dropdowns escaping overflow/z-index.
Often portals to document.body or a dedicated #modal-root.

lines: document.body // render here, outside the parent DOM
code.explanation: The modal's DOM attaches to document.body (escaping parent overflow/z-index), but it stays a React child of whatever rendered <Modal />.

diagram.caption: Portal: same React tree, different DOM location.

commonMistakes: Assuming events do not bubble to React ancestors (they do, via the React tree).
Forgetting the target DOM node must exist before rendering the portal.
Using a portal when a normal nested element would suffice.

exercises.prompt: A button inside a portal has an onClick. The component that created the portal also has an onClick wrapper. Does the click reach the wrapper?

solution.explanation: Yes. Even though the DOM node is elsewhere, events bubble along the React tree, so the ancestor's handler in the React hierarchy still fires.

checkYourself: What problem do portals solve for modals?
Along which tree do portal events bubble?

concepts.title: lazy + Suspense for component-level code splitting
concepts.summary: React.lazy loads a component's code only when it is first rendered, and Suspense shows a fallback while that chunk loads. Together they split your bundle so users download code on demand.
concepts.explanation: Large apps benefit from code splitting: instead of shipping everything upfront, you load parts when needed. const Heavy = lazy(() => import('./Heavy')) tells React to fetch that module the first time Heavy renders. Because loading is async, you wrap the lazy component in <Suspense fallback={...}> to show a spinner or skeleton until the code arrives. This reduces initial bundle size and speeds up first load. Common for routes, modals, tabs, and rarely-used heavy components (charts, editors). The dynamic import() must resolve to a module with a default export.
concepts.analogy: lazy + Suspense is like a streaming service: you do not download the whole library at once; each show loads when you press play, with a brief 'loading' spinner.
keyPoints: lazy(() => import('./X')) defers loading X's code until first render.
Wrap it in <Suspense fallback={...}> for the loading state.
Shrinks the initial bundle; loads code on demand.
Great for routes, modals, tabs, heavy widgets.
The imported module needs a default export.

lines: const Chart = lazy(() => import('./Chart')); // separate chunk
code.explanation: Chart's code is only fetched when the user clicks to show it. Suspense displays a fallback during the download.

commonMistakes: Rendering a lazy component without a Suspense boundary above it.
Importing a module whose component is a named (not default) export.
Over-splitting tiny components, adding request overhead.

exercises.prompt: Lazy-load a Settings component and wrap it with a Suspense fallback of 'Loading...'.

solution.explanation: lazy defers Settings' code; Suspense supplies the fallback while the chunk loads.

checkYourself: What does lazy do to a component's code?
Why is a Suspense boundary required around a lazy component?

concepts.title: Suspense: fallback UI and what it suspends on
concepts.summary: Suspense lets a part of the tree 'wait' for something (lazy code, or data with supported sources) and display a fallback until it is ready. It declaratively coordinates loading states for everything inside the boundary.
concepts.explanation: A <Suspense fallback={...}> boundary catches the 'I am not ready yet' signal from any suspending child and renders the fallback instead, swapping to the real content once everything resolves. What can suspend? Lazy components (their code chunk), and data reads from Suspense-enabled sources (frameworks, the use() hook reading a promise, certain libraries). A boundary can wrap multiple suspending children and will wait for all of them before revealing content, so you can group related loading into one fallback. You can nest boundaries to reveal stable parts first and keep finer fallbacks for slower pieces.
concepts.analogy: Suspense is like a restaurant that waits until your whole table's order is ready before serving, showing a 'preparing your meal' card in the meantime - and you can split into courses (nested boundaries) if you prefer.
keyPoints: <Suspense fallback> shows a placeholder until children are ready.
Suspends on: lazy code, and data from Suspense-enabled sources (e.g. use() with a promise).
Waits for ALL suspending children in the boundary before revealing.
Nest boundaries to control what appears first.
Declarative loading: no manual isLoading flags inside the boundary.

code.explanation: If either Profile or Feed suspends, the Skeleton shows until both are ready. They share one coordinated loading state.

diagram.caption: Suspense shows the fallback until every suspending child inside it resolves.

commonMistakes: Expecting a plain useEffect fetch to trigger Suspense (it does not by itself).
Wrapping too much in one boundary, so one slow item blocks everything.
Forgetting that all children in a boundary reveal together.

exercises.prompt: Two components in one Suspense boundary; one resolves fast, one slow. When does the real content appear?

solution.explanation: Only when BOTH have resolved - the boundary reveals its children together. To show the fast one sooner, put the slow one in its own nested boundary.

checkYourself: What can cause a component to suspend?
How do nested boundaries change what users see first?

concepts.title: Profiler: measuring render performance
concepts.summary: The Profiler measures how often and how long components render, helping you find performance bottlenecks. It exists both as a React DevTools tab and as a <Profiler> component with an onRender callback.
concepts.explanation: Before optimizing, measure. The React DevTools Profiler records commits and shows which components rendered, how long each took, and why. For programmatic measurement, wrap part of the tree in <Profiler id="..." onRender={callback}>; React calls the callback after each commit with timing data (the phase 'mount' or 'update', actualDuration, etc.). Use it to confirm a component is actually slow or re-rendering too often before reaching for memo/useMemo. Profiling adds a little overhead, so use it during development, not in production hot paths.
concepts.analogy: The Profiler is like a stopwatch and lap counter for your components - you find out who is actually slow before you start 'training' (optimizing) the wrong runner.
keyPoints: Measures render frequency and duration to find bottlenecks.
Available as a DevTools tab and a <Profiler> component.
<Profiler id onRender> reports phase and timings per commit.
Measure before optimizing - avoid guessing.
Profiling has overhead; use it in development.

lines: console.log(id, phase, actualDuration); // ms spent rendering
code.explanation: After each commit of the Sidebar subtree, onRender logs whether it was a mount or update and how long it took to render.

commonMistakes: Optimizing without measuring first (premature optimization).
Leaving Profiler instrumentation in production hot paths.
Misreading actualDuration as total app time rather than render time for that subtree.

exercises.prompt: What is the right first step before adding React.memo and useMemo everywhere?

solution.explanation: Measure with the Profiler. Confirm which components actually render too often or too slowly, then apply targeted memoization where it helps.

checkYourself: What two forms does the Profiler take?
Why measure before memoizing?

concepts.title: Composition over inheritance
concepts.summary: React favors composition - building components by combining and nesting other components - over class inheritance. You reuse code by passing children and props, not by extending base components.
concepts.explanation: In many OOP frameworks you reuse UI by subclassing. React deliberately does not: components reuse behavior by composition. If a specialized component needs the look of a generic one, it RENDERS the generic one and fills it via children or props, rather than inheriting from it. For example, a Dialog and an AlertDialog both use a generic Modal by composing it, passing different content. This keeps relationships explicit and flexible - you can mix and match pieces - and avoids the rigidity and tight coupling of deep inheritance hierarchies. The official guidance: there is no use case where component inheritance is recommended over composition.
concepts.analogy: Composition is building with LEGO bricks you snap together; inheritance is carving a statue from one block where every change means re-carving. Bricks rearrange freely.
keyPoints: Reuse via composition (children/props), not class inheritance.
Specialized components render generic ones and fill them in.
Relationships stay explicit and flexible.
Avoids tight coupling of inheritance hierarchies.
React's official stance: prefer composition in all cases.

code.explanation: ConfirmDialog reuses Modal by rendering it and supplying content - no subclassing. Other dialogs compose Modal the same way.

commonMistakes: Trying to extend a component class to specialize it.
Building deep inheritance chains instead of small composable pieces.
Overloading one component with flags instead of composing variants.

exercises.prompt: Given a generic Card, create a ProfileCard by composition that puts an avatar and name inside it.

solution.explanation: ProfileCard composes Card and fills it via children - reuse without inheritance.

checkYourself: How do you specialize a generic component in React?
Why does React avoid inheritance for UI reuse?

concepts.title: Render props
concepts.summary: A render prop is a prop whose value is a function that returns JSX. The component calls it to let the consumer decide what to render, while the component itself manages some state or behavior.
concepts.explanation: The render-props pattern shares behavior by passing a function that the component invokes with internal data. A component (say, a MouseTracker) holds some state and calls props.render(state) (or uses children as a function) so the caller renders whatever they want with that state. This was a popular way to share logic before hooks; it keeps the behavior in one component while leaving the UI to the consumer. Today custom hooks usually express the same reuse more cleanly, but render props remain useful and you will see them in libraries (e.g., things that expose values via a function child).
concepts.analogy: A render prop is like a function that does the measuring and then hands you the numbers, saying 'you decide how to display them'. The tool gathers data; you draw the chart.
keyPoints: A prop (often children) is a function returning JSX.
The component calls it with internal state/behavior.
Consumer controls the rendered output; component owns the logic.
Pre-hooks pattern for sharing behavior; hooks often replace it.
Still common in libraries that expose values via a function child.

lines: <div onMouseMove={(e) => setPos({ x: e.clientX, y: e.clientY })}>
{children(pos)} {/* call the render prop with state */}
code.explanation: MouseTracker tracks the pointer and calls children(pos). The consumer's function decides how to render that position.

commonMistakes: Forgetting that children is a function here, not elements.
Reaching for render props where a custom hook is simpler today.
Creating a new render function each render and harming memoized children.

exercises.prompt: Write a Toggle component that exposes { on, toggle } to its children via a render prop.

solution.explanation: Toggle owns the boolean and exposes it plus a toggler through the function child; the consumer renders the button.

checkYourself: What is the value of a render prop?
What modern feature often replaces render props?

concepts.title: Higher-Order Components (HOC)
concepts.summary: A Higher-Order Component is a function that takes a component and returns a new component with added behavior or props. It is a pattern for reusing logic by wrapping, common before hooks.
concepts.explanation: An HOC is just a function: withSomething(Component) returns an enhanced component that renders the original with extra props or behavior injected (e.g., withAuth injects the current user, withLogger logs renders). The convention is to name them with a 'with' prefix and to pass through unrelated props. HOCs were the dominant code-reuse pattern in the class era; hooks now cover most cases more directly without the wrapping layers and 'wrapper hell'. You still encounter HOCs in older code and some libraries (e.g., connect from older Redux). Forward refs and copy static methods if you need full transparency.
concepts.analogy: An HOC is like gift-wrapping: you take a plain box (component) and wrap it with extra features (ribbon, label). The contents are the same; the wrapper adds capabilities.
keyPoints: HOC = function(Component) => enhanced Component.
Injects extra props/behavior by wrapping.
Naming convention: withX; pass through other props.
Pre-hooks reuse pattern; hooks usually replace it.
Watch for wrapper nesting, ref forwarding, and static methods.

lines: // <LoggedButton onClick={...} /> behaves like Button but logs on mount
code.explanation: withLogger returns a new component that wraps Button, adds logging, and forwards all props - the essence of the HOC pattern.

commonMistakes: Not forwarding props (or refs), breaking the wrapped component.
Stacking many HOCs into deep, hard-to-debug wrapper trees.
Choosing an HOC where a custom hook would be cleaner today.

exercises.prompt: Write a withLoading HOC that shows 'Loading...' when an isLoading prop is true, else renders the wrapped component.

solution.explanation: The HOC intercepts isLoading and conditionally renders a fallback or the wrapped component with the remaining props.

checkYourself: What does an HOC take and return?
Why must an HOC usually forward props (and sometimes refs)?

concepts.title: Compound components
concepts.summary: Compound components are a set of components designed to work together as a unit, sharing implicit state (often via Context). The consumer composes the parts, e.g. <Tabs><Tab/><TabPanel/></Tabs>, with a clean, expressive API.
concepts.explanation: Instead of one component with dozens of props, you expose several related components that coordinate behind the scenes. A parent (Tabs) holds the shared state and provides it via Context; child parts (TabList, Tab, TabPanel) read that context to know which tab is active. The consumer arranges the pieces declaratively, getting flexibility in structure and ordering while the implementation handles the wiring. This pattern powers many UI libraries (menus, accordions, selects). It trades a few more components for a much more readable and flexible API than a single mega-component.
concepts.analogy: Compound components are like the parts of a single appliance set - the remote, the base, the screen - sold to work together. You arrange them, and they coordinate internally.
keyPoints: Several components that work together as one feature.
Shared state lives in a parent, passed via Context.
Consumer composes the parts declaratively.
Cleaner than one component with a huge prop list.
Common for tabs, menus, accordions, selects.

code.explanation: Tabs holds the active index in Context; each Tab reads and updates it. The consumer just arranges Tab elements.

commonMistakes: Passing shared state via props through every child instead of Context.
Making the API too implicit, so usage is unclear without docs.
Forgetting to handle the case where a part is used outside its parent provider.

exercises.prompt: How do compound components usually share state between the parent and its parts?

solution.explanation: Through Context: the parent provides shared state, and each part consumes it with useContext, avoiding prop threading and keeping the API declarative.

checkYourself: What problem do compound components solve compared to one big component?
Where does their shared state live?

concepts.title: Provider pattern
concepts.summary: The Provider pattern wraps a subtree in a Context Provider (often bundled with its own state logic and a custom hook) to supply shared data and actions to any descendant. It centralizes cross-cutting state like theme, auth, or a cart.
concepts.explanation: You create a dedicated provider component that owns some state (and the functions to change it) and exposes it through Context, plus a custom hook for ergonomic access. Descendants call the hook (e.g. useTheme(), useAuth()) to read state and trigger actions without prop drilling. This packages a slice of app state with its logic in one place and gives consumers a clean API. It is the idiomatic way to share app-wide concerns in plain React (theme/locale/auth/cart), and is the conceptual basis many state libraries build upon. Beware of putting fast-changing values in a single provider, which can re-render many consumers.
concepts.analogy: A provider is like a utility company for a neighborhood: it generates the resource (state) centrally and pipes it to every house (consumer) that subscribes, with a simple tap (the custom hook) to access it.
keyPoints: A provider component owns state + actions and exposes them via Context.
A paired custom hook (useX) gives clean access.
Descendants read/update without prop drilling.
Idiomatic for theme, auth, locale, cart, etc.
Avoid putting volatile values in one provider (wide re-renders).

lines: const value = useMemo(() => ({ user, login, logout }), [user]);
code.explanation: AuthProvider bundles user state with login/logout and exposes them via Context; useAuth gives any component a clean way to access them. The value is memoized to avoid needless consumer re-renders.

diagram.caption: A provider supplies state and actions through Context; a custom hook gives consumers clean access.

commonMistakes: Recreating the value object inline each render (memoize it).
Stuffing fast-changing values in one provider, re-rendering all consumers.
Skipping the custom hook, leaving consumers to call useContext directly everywhere.

exercises.prompt: Sketch a ThemeProvider exposing { theme, toggleTheme } and a useTheme hook.

solution.explanation: The provider owns theme and the toggler, exposes them via a memoized value, and useTheme gives consumers a clean API.

checkYourself: What does a provider component bundle together?
Why memoize the Context value object?

concepts.title: Virtual DOM
concepts.summary: The Virtual DOM is a lightweight in-memory representation of the UI as plain JavaScript objects. React renders to this virtual tree first, compares it with the previous one, and applies only the minimal real-DOM changes.
concepts.explanation: Direct DOM manipulation is expensive and error-prone. Instead, when a component renders, React produces a tree of plain objects (React elements) describing the UI - the Virtual DOM. On an update, React builds a new virtual tree and diffs it against the previous virtual tree to compute the smallest set of real DOM mutations needed, then commits them. The Virtual DOM is not magic speed - it is a strategy that makes declarative updates practical and batched. It also decouples your code from the DOM, enabling other renderers (React Native, etc.). It is a description layer, not the browser DOM itself.
concepts.analogy: The Virtual DOM is like editing a draft on paper first, marking only the changed lines, then sending just those edits to the printer - instead of reprinting the whole document every time.
keyPoints: An in-memory tree of plain JS objects describing the UI.
React diffs new vs previous virtual tree to find minimal changes.
Only the necessary real-DOM mutations are committed.
Enables declarative updates and batching; not inherently 'fast magic'.
Decouples components from the DOM (allows other renderers).

diagram.caption: React diffs the new virtual tree against the previous one and applies only the differences to the real DOM.

commonMistakes: Believing the Virtual DOM is always faster than direct DOM writes.
Confusing React elements (objects) with real DOM nodes.
Assuming a re-render always means heavy DOM work (the diff may be a no-op).

exercises.prompt: Is the Virtual DOM the same as the browser's DOM?

solution.explanation: No. The Virtual DOM is a lightweight JavaScript description of the UI. React diffs it to decide the minimal real-DOM changes; the browser DOM is the actual rendered tree.

checkYourself: What is the Virtual DOM made of?
Why does React diff two virtual trees?

concepts.title: Reconciliation and the diffing algorithm
concepts.summary: Reconciliation is the process of comparing the new element tree with the previous one to decide what to update. React uses heuristics: different element types replace the subtree, and keys match list items across renders.
concepts.explanation: A general tree-diff would be too slow, so React uses an O(n) heuristic algorithm. Core rules: (1) If an element's type changes (div -> span, or ComponentA -> ComponentB), React tears down the old subtree and builds a new one - state is lost. (2) If the type is the same, React keeps the node and updates only changed attributes/props, then recurses into children. (3) For lists, keys tell React which child is which, so it can reorder/insert/remove efficiently and preserve each item's state. Understanding reconciliation explains why type changes reset state, why keys matter, and why moving a component to a different position can remount it.
concepts.analogy: Reconciliation is like a smart proofreader comparing two drafts: same paragraph type -> just fix the words; different type -> rewrite that section; and name tags (keys) let them track paragraphs even when reordered.
keyPoints: O(n) heuristic diff, not a full tree comparison.
Different element type -> replace subtree (state lost).
Same type -> update props in place, recurse into children.
Keys identify list items for efficient reorder/insert/remove.
Explains state resets on type change and the importance of keys.

lines:
// Switching between and

unmounts one and mounts the other.
// Same type, changed prop -> in-place update, state preserved:
code.explanation: When the element type differs between renders, reconciliation replaces the node (losing state). When the type matches, it patches props in place.

commonMistakes: Conditionally swapping element types and being surprised state resets.
Relying on index keys, which misalign items when lists change.
Assuming React deeply diffs unrelated subtrees (it stops at type mismatches).

exercises.prompt: You render <Counter /> inside an <div> on one render and inside a <section> on the next (same position). Does Counter keep its state?

solution.explanation: No. The parent wrapper type changed (div -> section), so React replaces that subtree and remounts Counter, resetting its state. Keep the wrapper type stable to preserve state.

checkYourself: What happens during reconciliation when an element's type changes?
How do keys help the diffing algorithm?

concepts.title: Fiber architecture
concepts.summary: Fiber is React's internal reimplementation of the reconciler (since React 16). It represents work as a linked list of 'fiber' units, enabling React to pause, resume, prioritize, and abort rendering work - the foundation for concurrent features.
concepts.explanation: Before Fiber, reconciliation was a single recursive pass that could not be interrupted - long renders blocked the main thread. Fiber breaks rendering into small units of work (one fiber per element), arranged as a linked structure React can traverse incrementally. This lets React do work in chunks, yield to the browser for high-priority tasks (like input), and resume later, or discard work that became stale. Fiber also tracks two trees (current and work-in-progress) and supports effects and priorities. You rarely touch fibers directly, but Fiber is why useTransition, useDeferredValue, Suspense, and interruptible rendering are possible.
concepts.analogy: Fiber is like turning one giant uninterruptible task into a stack of small index cards. React can put the stack down to answer an urgent call (user input), then pick it back up - or toss outdated cards.
keyPoints: Fiber = React's incremental reconciler (React 16+).
Work is split into small units (fibers) React can pause/resume.
Enables prioritization, yielding to the browser, and aborting stale work.
Maintains current and work-in-progress trees.
Foundation for concurrent rendering (transitions, Suspense).

diagram.caption: Fiber processes rendering in small units, yielding to the browser for urgent work between them.

commonMistakes: Thinking Fiber made rendering synchronously faster (it makes it interruptible/schedulable).
Trying to manipulate fiber internals in app code.
Assuming concurrency is automatic everywhere (you opt in via transitions/Suspense).

exercises.prompt: What capability did Fiber unlock that the old recursive reconciler lacked?

solution.explanation: Interruptible, prioritizable rendering: React can pause work, let the browser handle urgent tasks, then resume or abort - enabling concurrent features like transitions and Suspense.

checkYourself: Why was the pre-Fiber reconciler limiting?
Name two features Fiber makes possible.

concepts.title: Render phase vs commit phase (deep)
concepts.summary: The render phase computes the work-in-progress tree and is pure and interruptible - React may pause, restart, or discard it. The commit phase applies changes to the DOM and runs synchronously and to completion, followed by layout effects then passive effects.
concepts.explanation: Going deeper than the basic model: in the RENDER phase React calls your components to build a work-in-progress fiber tree and diff it. Because this phase can be paused, restarted, or thrown away (especially under concurrency), it MUST be pure - no side effects, no DOM mutations, no subscriptions. React may even call components multiple times. In the COMMIT phase React mutates the real DOM in one synchronous, uninterruptible pass; then it runs useLayoutEffect (synchronously, before paint), the browser paints, and finally useEffect (passive, after paint). This split is why side effects belong in effects, not render, and why render functions can run more than once.
concepts.analogy: Render is sketching and re-sketching blueprints (can be redone freely, no concrete poured). Commit is the construction crew building exactly what the final blueprint says, all at once, no interruptions.
keyPoints: Render phase: build/diff the WIP tree; pure and interruptible; may run more than once.
No side effects or DOM writes during render.
Commit phase: synchronous, uninterruptible DOM mutations.
Order after commit: layout effects (pre-paint) -> paint -> passive effects.
Justifies putting effects (not render) where side effects live.

lines: RP[Render phase: pure, interruptible] --> CP[Commit phase: sync DOM]
diagram.caption: Render (interruptible, pure) then commit (synchronous), with layout effects before paint and passive effects after.

commonMistakes: Performing side effects during render, which breaks under concurrency.
Assuming a component renders exactly once per update (it may not).
Confusing useLayoutEffect (pre-paint) with useEffect (post-paint) timing.

exercises.prompt: Why must the render phase be free of side effects?

solution.explanation: Because React can pause, restart, or discard render work (and may call components multiple times). Side effects there would run inconsistently or repeatedly. Effects run in/after commit, which is stable.

checkYourself: Which phase is interruptible, and which is synchronous?
In what order do layout effects, paint, and passive effects occur?

concepts.title: Bailout: how React skips a re-render
concepts.summary: A bailout is when React decides it can skip re-rendering a component (and its subtree) because nothing relevant changed. It happens when a component re-renders to the identical element references or passes memoization checks.
concepts.explanation: React has optimizations to avoid unnecessary work. If a component is asked to render but its state did not change and it returns the same element references as before, React can bail out and reuse the previous result, sometimes skipping children too. With React.memo, a bailout occurs when props are shallowly equal. Another subtle case: if you pass the SAME element (e.g., children passed through) and the child's props/state did not change, React can skip re-rendering that child even when the parent re-rendered - which is one reason passing children as props can reduce renders. Bailouts are automatic; you enable more of them by keeping props/state references stable.
concepts.analogy: A bailout is like a teacher skipping re-grading an essay that has not changed since last time - same paper, same grade, no need to redo the work.
keyPoints: Bailout = React skips rendering because nothing relevant changed.
Happens on unchanged state returning identical element references.
React.memo bails out when props are shallowly equal.
Passing stable children can let React skip child renders.
Stable references enable more bailouts.

lines: // Passing children as a prop can enable a bailout for <Slow>:
{children} {/* same element reference -> may skip re-render */}
// <Layout><Slow /></Layout>: clicking re-renders Layout but Slow can bail out
code.explanation: Because <Slow /> is created by the parent of Layout and passed as children, its element reference is unchanged when Layout's own state updates, so React can skip re-rendering Slow.

commonMistakes: Expecting bailouts while passing freshly-created props each render.
Assuming memo guarantees a skip even when props change.
Not realizing children-as-props can avoid re-rendering expensive subtrees.

exercises.prompt: A memoized child receives only primitive props that did not change, but the parent re-rendered. Does the child render again?

solution.explanation: No - React.memo bails out because the props are shallowly equal, so the child reuses its previous render even though the parent re-rendered.

checkYourself: What is a bailout?
How can passing children as props reduce re-renders?

concepts.title: Automatic batching (React 18)
concepts.summary: Automatic batching means React groups multiple state updates into a single re-render, even inside promises, timeouts, and native event handlers. Before React 18, batching only happened inside React event handlers.
concepts.explanation: Batching combines several setState calls into one render for efficiency and to avoid intermediate UI states. In React 17 and earlier, this batching applied only within React's synthetic event handlers; updates in setTimeout, promises (.then/async), or native events each caused their own render. React 18's automatic batching extends batching to all these contexts by default when using createRoot. The result: fewer renders and more consistent behavior. If you ever need an update to flush immediately and separately (rare), you can opt out with flushSync. Updater functions still apply in order within a batch.
concepts.analogy: Automatic batching is like a delivery service that now waits to combine all your same-day orders into one trip, whereas before it only combined orders placed at the counter, not those phoned in later.
keyPoints: Batching merges multiple updates into one re-render.
React 18: batches in promises, timeouts, and native handlers too.
Pre-18: only React event handlers were batched.
Requires createRoot (React 18 root API).
Opt out for a single sync flush with flushSync (rarely needed).

lines: setB(2); // React 18: these are batched -> ONE re-render
code.explanation: Inside a timeout, React 18 batches setA and setB into a single re-render. In React 17 each would have triggered its own render.

commonMistakes: Assuming pre-18 behavior where async updates were not batched.
Reaching for flushSync routinely (it defeats batching and hurts performance).
Forgetting that legacy ReactDOM.render does not enable the new batching.

exercises.prompt: In React 18, inside a .then() callback you call three setters. How many re-renders?

solution.explanation: One. Automatic batching groups all three updates from the same tick into a single re-render, even though they are inside a promise callback.

checkYourself: What contexts did React 18 add batching to?
How do you force a single synchronous flush when you truly need it?

concepts.title: useTransition and startTransition for non-urgent updates
concepts.summary: useTransition lets you mark some state updates as non-urgent 'transitions' so React can keep the UI responsive, rendering them in the background while urgent updates (like typing) stay snappy. It also gives an isPending flag.
concepts.explanation: Some updates are urgent (showing what the user just typed) and others can wait (re-filtering a huge list based on that text). useTransition returns [isPending, startTransition]. Wrapping the heavy update in startTransition(() => setResults(...)) tells React it is low-priority and interruptible: React renders it in the background, can pause it for urgent work, and discards it if a newer transition arrives. isPending is true while the transition is rendering, so you can show a subtle loading indicator without blocking input. Use it to prevent janky typing/navigation when expensive renders follow user input. startTransition can also be imported standalone when you do not need isPending.
concepts.analogy: It is like a chef who immediately serves the quick appetizer (urgent update) and lets the slow main course cook in the background (transition), checking on it between urgent tasks - and starting over if you change your order.
keyPoints: useTransition() -> [isPending, startTransition].
Wrap non-urgent updates in startTransition to keep the UI responsive.
Transitions are interruptible and can be discarded if superseded.
isPending lets you show a lightweight loading state.
Urgent updates (input) are not blocked by the transition's heavy render.

code.explanation: Typing updates the input urgently; the expensive filter runs as an interruptible transition, so keystrokes never feel laggy. isPending shows a subtle indicator.

commonMistakes: Wrapping the urgent input update itself in startTransition (then the field feels laggy).
Using transitions for genuinely fast updates (no benefit).
Expecting transitions to make the heavy work faster - they make it non-blocking, not quicker.

exercises.prompt: Which update should be wrapped in startTransition: the controlled input's setQuery, or the expensive setResults filter?

solution.explanation: The expensive setResults filter. setQuery must stay urgent so typing is instant; the heavy, derived update is the non-urgent transition.

checkYourself: What does useTransition return?
What does marking an update as a transition allow React to do?

concepts.title: useDeferredValue for deferring a displayed value
concepts.summary: useDeferredValue returns a copy of a value that 'lags behind' during urgent updates, letting expensive UI that depends on it render at a lower priority. It is a simpler alternative to useTransition when you only have a value, not the update call.
concepts.explanation: Sometimes you receive a fast-changing value (a prop or state) and an expensive component derives from it, but you cannot wrap the original setState yourself. useDeferredValue(value) gives you a deferred version: during urgent renders it returns the previous value, then updates to the latest in a background, interruptible render. You feed the deferred value to the heavy component, so it re-renders at low priority while the rest of the UI (showing the fresh value) stays responsive. You can compare value !== deferredValue to show a 'stale' indicator. It is conceptually like a built-in debounce/throttle handled by React's scheduler rather than a timer.
concepts.analogy: useDeferredValue is like a TV with a slight broadcast delay on one channel: the live feed (urgent value) is instant, while the heavy graphics overlay (expensive render) catches up a beat later, smoothly.
keyPoints: Returns a deferred copy that lags during urgent updates.
Feed it to expensive components to lower their render priority.
Useful when you have a value but not the setter to wrap.
Compare value vs deferredValue to show a stale state.
Scheduler-based, not a timer-based debounce.

lines: const deferred = useDeferredValue(text); // lags during fast typing
code.explanation: The input stays responsive using text, while ExpensiveList uses the deferred value, so its costly render does not block keystrokes.

commonMistakes: Passing the fresh value (not the deferred one) to the expensive component.
Expecting it to reduce the cost of the heavy render rather than its priority.
Using it where a transition around the actual setter would be clearer.

exercises.prompt: You get a rapidly changing prop and an expensive chart derives from it; you cannot change where the prop is set. Which API fits?

solution.explanation: useDeferredValue. You wrap the incoming value and pass the deferred copy to the chart, lowering its render priority without controlling the original setState.

checkYourself: When is useDeferredValue preferable to useTransition?
How can you detect that the deferred value is stale?

concepts.title: How concurrency makes renders interruptible
concepts.summary: Concurrent rendering lets React prepare multiple versions of the UI without blocking the main thread. It can start rendering, pause to handle urgent work, resume, or throw away an in-progress render that became outdated.
concepts.explanation: Built on Fiber, concurrent React treats rendering as interruptible work managed by a scheduler. When a low-priority update is rendering and something urgent happens (user input, a higher-priority update), React can pause the in-progress render, let the browser stay responsive, then continue - or discard the partial work if newer state makes it irrelevant. Crucially, in-progress renders are not committed to the DOM until they finish, so the user never sees a half-rendered tree. This is opt-in via features like transitions, useDeferredValue, and Suspense; you do not get torn or partial UI. The practical payoff is a responsive interface even when expensive updates are in flight.
concepts.analogy: Concurrency is like a chef preparing a dish off to the side: if a VIP order comes in, they pause, handle it, then return - and they never plate a half-finished dish; you only see completed plates.
keyPoints: Rendering becomes interruptible work under a scheduler (via Fiber).
React can pause, resume, or abort in-progress renders.
Partial renders are never committed - no torn UI.
Opt in through transitions, useDeferredValue, and Suspense.
Goal: keep the UI responsive during expensive updates.

lines: Int -->|yes| Pause[Pause + handle urgent] --> Resume[Resume or restart]
diagram.caption: Concurrent rendering can pause for urgent work, resume, or drop stale work; only completed renders commit.

commonMistakes: Assuming concurrency is on automatically for all updates (it is opt-in).
Doing side effects during render, which breaks when renders restart.
Expecting to see intermediate renders (you do not - only completed ones commit).

exercises.prompt: During a paused, in-progress concurrent render, can the user see a half-rendered tree?

solution.explanation: No. React does not commit incomplete renders to the DOM. The user only ever sees fully completed renders; partial work stays off-screen until done or is discarded.

checkYourself: What can React do with an in-progress render when urgent work arrives?
Why must render stay pure for concurrency to work?

concepts.title: Suspense for data fetching
concepts.summary: Suspense can coordinate loading states for data, not just code. A component reads data from a Suspense-enabled source; while it is not ready, the nearest boundary shows a fallback, removing manual isLoading flags.
concepts.explanation: Beyond lazy code, Suspense integrates with data fetching when the data source is Suspense-aware - frameworks (like those built on RSC), Suspense-enabled libraries, or the use() hook reading a promise. The component 'reads' data and, if it is pending, suspends; the boundary renders the fallback until it resolves. This shifts loading UI from scattered isLoading/isError state into declarative boundaries, and pairs with concurrent features to avoid jarring spinner flashes (transitions can keep showing old content while new data loads). Note: a plain useEffect fetch does NOT automatically trigger Suspense - you need a Suspense-compatible mechanism.
concepts.analogy: Suspense for data is like a kitchen ticket system tied to ingredients: the dish (component) cannot be plated until its ingredients (data) arrive, and the 'preparing' card (fallback) shows automatically until they do.
keyPoints: Suspense coordinates data loading when the source is Suspense-enabled.
Components read data and suspend until it resolves.
Moves loading UI into declarative boundaries (no scattered isLoading).
Works via frameworks, Suspense-ready libraries, or use() with a promise.
Plain useEffect fetches do not trigger Suspense by themselves.

lines: const user = use(userPromise); // suspends until resolved
code.explanation: Profile reads a promise with use(); while it is pending, the Suspense boundary shows the fallback. No manual loading state is needed in Profile.

commonMistakes: Expecting a regular useEffect+fetch to suspend (it will not).
Creating the promise inside the component on every render (recreate-and-suspend loop).
Forgetting an error boundary alongside Suspense for rejected data.

exercises.prompt: Does fetching data in a useEffect automatically make a component suspend?

solution.explanation: No. Suspense needs a Suspense-enabled data source (a framework integration, a Suspense-ready library, or use() reading a promise). A bare useEffect fetch uses ordinary loading state instead.

checkYourself: What does a component do to 'suspend' on data?
Why pair Suspense with an error boundary?

concepts.title: Streaming SSR via Suspense
concepts.summary: Streaming SSR sends HTML to the browser in chunks as it becomes ready, using Suspense boundaries as the unit of streaming. Slow parts stream their fallback first, then their real content streams in when ready.
concepts.explanation: Traditional SSR rendered the whole page to a string before sending anything - one slow data dependency delayed the entire response. With streaming SSR (renderToPipeableStream on Node, renderToReadableStream on web runtimes), React streams the shell immediately and uses Suspense boundaries to defer slow sections: each boundary's fallback is sent first, and as that section finishes on the server, React streams its HTML plus a small script to slot it in. Combined with selective hydration, users see meaningful content sooner and can interact with ready parts while others are still loading. This dramatically improves perceived performance for data-heavy pages.
concepts.analogy: Streaming SSR is like a restaurant serving courses as they are ready instead of making you wait for the entire meal - bread arrives immediately, the slow-cooked main follows when done.
keyPoints: HTML streams in chunks as it becomes ready, not all at once.
Suspense boundaries are the units of streaming.
Fallbacks stream first; real content streams in when resolved.
APIs: renderToPipeableStream (Node), renderToReadableStream (web).
Pairs with selective hydration for faster interactivity.

lines: Shell[Stream shell + fallbacks] --> Ready{Section ready on server?}
Chunk --> Slot[Script slots it into place]
diagram.caption: The shell and fallbacks stream first; each Suspense section streams in when the server finishes it.

commonMistakes: Using renderToString (no streaming) for data-heavy pages and blocking on slow data.
Putting everything in one Suspense boundary, defeating progressive streaming.
Confusing server streaming APIs with the client createRoot/hydrateRoot APIs.

exercises.prompt: What determines which parts of the page can stream in separately?

solution.explanation: Suspense boundaries. Each boundary streams its fallback first and its real HTML when ready, so the page arrives progressively instead of waiting for the slowest section.

checkYourself: How do Suspense boundaries relate to streaming SSR?
Name the Node and web streaming render APIs.

concepts.title: Strategy for placing Suspense boundaries
concepts.summary: Place Suspense boundaries to match meaningful loading regions: wrap independent sections separately so fast content shows first, group related content under one boundary, and avoid one giant boundary that blocks the whole page.
concepts.explanation: Boundary placement shapes the loading experience. Guidelines: (1) Reveal the shell fast - keep the outer layout outside boundaries so navigation and structure appear immediately. (2) Give slow, independent sections their own boundaries so a slow widget does not hold up the rest. (3) Group content that should appear together under one boundary to avoid a popcorn effect of many spinners. (4) Provide fallbacks that preserve layout (skeletons matching size) to prevent layout shift. (5) Nest boundaries to reveal coarse content first and finer pieces as they load. (6) Pair with transitions so navigations can keep showing the old screen until the new content is ready, avoiding fallback flashes.
concepts.analogy: Boundary placement is like planning seating sections in a theater: open the doors to the lobby immediately, seat ready groups together, and do not make the whole audience wait outside because one group is late.
keyPoints: Keep the layout shell outside boundaries for an instant frame.
Isolate slow, independent sections in their own boundaries.
Group content meant to appear together under one boundary.
Use layout-preserving skeleton fallbacks to avoid shift.
Nest boundaries and pair with transitions to avoid spinner flashes.

code.explanation: The layout renders right away; Header and Feed each have their own boundary, so a slow Feed does not delay the Header, and skeletons keep the layout stable.

commonMistakes: Wrapping the entire page in one boundary, blocking everything on the slowest part.
Using mismatched fallbacks that cause layout shift.
Forgetting transitions, so navigations flash fallbacks instead of keeping old content.

exercises.prompt: A page has a fast header and a slow data feed. One boundary around both, or separate boundaries?

solution.explanation: Separate boundaries. That lets the header appear immediately while the slow feed shows its own fallback, instead of the whole page waiting on the feed.

checkYourself: Why keep the layout shell outside Suspense boundaries?
How do skeleton fallbacks help the experience?

concepts.title: useSyncExternalStore: subscribing to an external store without tearing
concepts.summary: useSyncExternalStore lets components safely read from an external (non-React) store and stay consistent during concurrent rendering, preventing 'tearing' where different parts of the UI show different versions of the same data.
concepts.explanation: State stored outside React (a custom store, a browser API, a third-party state library) needs a safe bridge so React re-renders on changes AND all components see the same value within a render - even when rendering is interruptible. useSyncExternalStore(subscribe, getSnapshot, getServerSnapshot?) takes: subscribe(callback) to register for change notifications (returns an unsubscribe), getSnapshot() to read the current value, and an optional getServerSnapshot() for SSR. React handles consistency, avoiding tearing that a naive useEffect subscription could cause under concurrency. It is primarily a tool for library authors (state managers, store integrations) and for subscribing to browser stores.
concepts.analogy: It is like a synchronized clock feed for a building: every screen subscribes to the same official time source and updates together, so no two clocks ever disagree (no tearing).
keyPoints: Bridges external stores to React with concurrency-safe reads.
Signature: useSyncExternalStore(subscribe, getSnapshot, getServerSnapshot?).
subscribe registers a change callback and returns an unsubscribe.
getSnapshot reads the current value; must be stable/consistent.
Prevents tearing; mainly for library authors and browser-store subscriptions.

code.explanation: useOnlineStatus subscribes to the browser's online/offline events and reads navigator.onLine consistently, with a server snapshot for SSR - all concurrency-safe.

commonMistakes: Returning a new object from getSnapshot each call (causes infinite loops); return stable references.
Forgetting getServerSnapshot in SSR, causing hydration mismatches.
Using a manual useEffect subscription where tearing can occur under concurrency.

exercises.prompt: What problem does useSyncExternalStore prevent that a naive useEffect subscription might not, under concurrent rendering?

solution.explanation: Tearing - where different components read different versions of the same external value within one render. useSyncExternalStore guarantees a consistent snapshot across the render.

checkYourself: What three arguments does useSyncExternalStore take?
Why must getSnapshot return stable values?

concepts.title: useInsertionEffect: injecting styles (CSS-in-JS)
concepts.summary: useInsertionEffect runs before any layout effects and before the DOM is mutated for layout reads, giving CSS-in-JS libraries a spot to inject <style> rules so layout measurements are correct. It is a tool for library authors, not app code.
concepts.explanation: CSS-in-JS libraries need to insert style rules into the document at exactly the right time: after React renders but before the browser computes layout, so that any useLayoutEffect reading sizes sees the styled result. useInsertionEffect fires earlier than useLayoutEffect and useEffect - before mutations are read for layout - which is the correct window to inject styles and avoid re-flows or wrong measurements. You should almost never use it in application code; it exists so styling libraries can integrate cleanly. Inside it you cannot read layout (refs are not yet meaningful for measurement). For everything app-level, use useEffect or useLayoutEffect.
concepts.analogy: useInsertionEffect is like the stagehands laying down the set's paint and markings before the actors (layout effects) step out to measure their positions - done in the brief window before anyone needs the stage.
keyPoints: Runs before layout effects and passive effects.
Purpose: inject <style> rules for CSS-in-JS at the right moment.
Keeps later layout measurements correct (no extra reflow).
Library-author tool; rarely used in app code.
Do not read layout inside it; use other effects for that.

lines: // Inside a CSS-in-JS library, not typical app code:
code.explanation: The style rule is injected in the insertion phase, before layout effects measure anything, so measurements reflect the injected styles.

commonMistakes: Using it in ordinary app components (almost never appropriate).
Trying to read element sizes/positions inside it.
Confusing its timing with useLayoutEffect (insertion runs earlier).

exercises.prompt: Who is the intended audience for useInsertionEffect, and what is it for?

solution.explanation: CSS-in-JS library authors. It provides the correct moment to inject <style> rules - before layout effects read layout - so measurements are accurate. App code should use useEffect/useLayoutEffect instead.

checkYourself: When does useInsertionEffect run relative to layout and passive effects?
Why should app code avoid it?

concepts.title: flushSync: forcing a synchronous DOM update
concepts.summary: flushSync(fn) forces React to flush the state updates inside fn and update the DOM synchronously, before flushSync returns. It opts out of automatic batching for a specific case where you need the DOM updated immediately.
concepts.explanation: Normally React 18 batches updates and commits asynchronously. Occasionally you must read or act on the updated DOM right after a state change - for example, updating state and then immediately measuring or scrolling to the new content, or integrating with non-React code that expects synchronous DOM. flushSync(() => setX(...)) makes React apply that update and mutate the DOM synchronously, so the next line sees the result. Use it sparingly: it bypasses batching and concurrency benefits and can hurt performance. It is imported from react-dom. Most code should rely on effects (useLayoutEffect) instead; reach for flushSync only when you genuinely need the DOM updated before the following statement.
concepts.analogy: flushSync is like demanding a rush print of one page right now, stepping out of the efficient batch-printing queue - handy occasionally, wasteful if you do it for everything.
keyPoints: flushSync(fn) flushes updates in fn and updates the DOM synchronously.
Opts out of automatic batching for that block.
Use when you must read/act on the updated DOM immediately.
Imported from react-dom.
Sparingly - it forfeits batching/concurrency benefits and can be slow.

lines: setItems((prev) => [...prev, item]); // DOM updates before returning
// Now the new item exists in the DOM and we can scroll to it:
code.explanation: flushSync commits the new item to the DOM synchronously, so the scrollIntoView call right after can find and scroll to the newly added element.

commonMistakes: Wrapping routine updates in flushSync, defeating batching.
Using it where useLayoutEffect would handle the post-commit work.
Importing it from 'react' instead of 'react-dom'.

exercises.prompt: Why might you wrap setItems in flushSync before calling scrollIntoView on the new item?

solution.explanation: So the DOM is updated synchronously and the new element exists before scrollIntoView runs. Without flushSync, the update may be batched/deferred and the element would not yet be in the DOM.

checkYourself: What does flushSync opt out of?
From which package is flushSync imported?

concepts.title: SSR and hydration: renderToString, renderToPipeableStream, hydrateRoot
concepts.summary: Server-Side Rendering produces HTML on the server so users see content fast; hydration then attaches React on the client to make that static HTML interactive. renderToString/renderToPipeableStream render on the server; hydrateRoot hydrates on the client.
concepts.explanation: SSR sends fully-formed HTML from the server so the page is visible (and SEO-friendly) before JavaScript loads. On the server you render with renderToString (simple, blocking) or renderToPipeableStream (streaming, recommended for data-heavy pages).
