The browser displays the HTML, then the client runs hydrateRoot(domNode, <App />), which reuses the existing DOM and attaches event listeners and state instead of recreating the markup. For hydration to succeed, the server and client must render the SAME output initially - mismatches (e.g., using Date.now(), random ids, or browser-only checks during render) cause hydration errors. After hydration, the app behaves like a normal client React app.
concepts.analogy: SSR is like delivering a fully built but unpowered model house (HTML); hydration is the electrician coming in to wire it up (events/state) so the switches actually work - using the house that is already there rather than rebuilding it.
keyPoints: SSR sends ready HTML for fast first paint and SEO.
Server APIs: renderToString (blocking), renderToPipeableStream (streaming).
Client: hydrateRoot reuses server HTML and attaches interactivity.
Server and client initial render must match (or hydration errors occur).
Avoid non-deterministic render output (Date.now, random, window checks).

code.explanation: The server streams App's HTML; the client hydrates the same #root markup, attaching React without rebuilding the DOM.

lines: S[Server renders HTML] --> B[Browser shows HTML]
diagram.caption: SSR sends HTML; the client hydrates it into a fully interactive app.

commonMistakes: Rendering non-deterministic output, causing hydration mismatches.
Using createRoot instead of hydrateRoot for server-rendered markup.
Blocking on renderToString for slow data instead of streaming.

exercises.prompt: Why must the server and client produce the same initial render?

solution.explanation: Hydration reuses the server HTML and attaches React to it. If the client's first render differs, React cannot match nodes correctly, causing hydration errors and possible UI glitches.

checkYourself: What does hydration add to server-rendered HTML?
Which client API hydrates (vs creates) a root?

concepts.title: Selective hydration
concepts.summary: Selective hydration lets React hydrate parts of the page independently and prioritize the parts the user interacts with first, instead of waiting to hydrate the whole tree before anything is interactive.
concepts.explanation: With streaming SSR and Suspense, React can hydrate the page in pieces rather than all at once. Sections wrapped in Suspense can hydrate independently as their code/data arrive, and React prioritizes hydrating whatever the user is trying to interact with - if someone clicks a not-yet-hydrated section, React hydrates that part first. This means a slow component no longer blocks interactivity for the rest of the page, and the time-to-interactive improves. Selective hydration is largely automatic when you use Suspense boundaries with streaming SSR; you benefit by structuring the page into sensible boundaries.
concepts.analogy: Selective hydration is like opening sections of a stadium as they are ready and rushing staff to whichever gate a fan is actually trying to enter, instead of keeping everyone outside until the entire stadium is staffed.
keyPoints: Hydrates page sections independently, not all at once.
Prioritizes hydrating the part the user interacts with first.
A slow section no longer blocks interactivity elsewhere.
Enabled by Suspense boundaries + streaming SSR.
Improves time-to-interactive; largely automatic.

lines: Click[User clicks B first] --> Prio[Hydrate B with priority]
diagram.caption: Sections hydrate independently; user interaction bumps a section's hydration priority.

commonMistakes: Expecting selective hydration without Suspense boundaries.
Assuming the whole app must hydrate before any interaction (it no longer must).
Putting everything in one boundary, preventing independent hydration.

exercises.prompt: A user clicks a section that has not hydrated yet. What does selective hydration do?

solution.explanation: React prioritizes hydrating that clicked section first, so it becomes interactive sooner, rather than hydrating strictly in order or waiting for the whole tree.

checkYourself: What enables selective hydration?
How does user interaction affect hydration order?

concepts.title: React Server Components: server vs client components, "use client" and "use server"
concepts.summary: React Server Components (RSC) run only on the server, render to a special format, and never ship their JavaScript to the browser. Client Components (marked with "use client") run in the browser and handle interactivity. "use server" marks server functions (Server Actions) callable from the client.
concepts.explanation: RSC splits components by where they run. Server Components execute on the server: they can access server resources (databases, the filesystem, secrets) directly, render to a serialized description, and send NO component JavaScript to the client - reducing bundle size. They cannot use state or effects or browser APIs. Client Components opt in with the "use client" directive at the top of the file; they ship to the browser and handle interactivity (useState, effects, events). You compose them: server components render client components where interactivity is needed and can pass serializable props. "use server" marks functions as Server Actions - server-side functions the client can invoke (e.g., from a form) without writing an API endpoint. RSC is typically used through a framework that wires up the bundler and server.
concepts.analogy: Server Components are like the kitchen staff who prepare dishes out of sight using ingredients only they can access; Client Components are the front-of-house staff who interact with guests. "use server" is a direct line guests can use to ask the kitchen to do something.
keyPoints: Server Components run only on the server; their JS is not sent to the client.
They can access server resources directly but cannot use state/effects/browser APIs.
"use client" marks Client Components that ship to and run in the browser.
Compose them: server renders client components, passing serializable props.
"use server" marks Server Actions the client can call (e.g., from forms).
Usually adopted via a framework that configures bundler + server.

lines: // Server Component (default in RSC): no 'use client'
const product = await db.getProduct(id); // direct server access
code.explanation: ProductPage runs on the server, queries the DB directly, and ships no JS for itself. It renders AddToCart, a Client Component ("use client") that handles interactivity in the browser.

lines: SC[Server Component: DB access, no client JS] --> Ser[Serialized output]
CC --> Browser[Runs in browser: state, events]
diagram.caption: Server Components render on the server and embed Client Components for interactivity in the browser.

commonMistakes: Using useState/useEffect or browser APIs in a Server Component.
Passing non-serializable props (functions, class instances) from server to client components.
Forgetting the "use client" directive must be the first line of the file.

exercises.prompt: Can a Server Component use useState? What directive turns a component into a Client Component?

solution.explanation: No - Server Components cannot use state, effects, or browser APIs. Adding the "use client" directive at the top of the file makes its components Client Components that run in the browser and can use state.

exercises.prompt: What is the purpose of the "use server" directive?

solution.explanation: It marks a function as a Server Action: a server-side function the client can call directly (e.g., from a form action) without manually building an API route.

checkYourself: What can Server Components do that Client Components cannot, and vice versa?
What do "use client" and "use server" each mark?

concepts.title: The serialization boundary and cache() for server data
concepts.summary: The serialization boundary is the line between server and client components: anything passed across it must be serializable (no functions or class instances, except Server Action references). cache() memoizes data-loading functions during a server render so repeated calls do not refetch.
concepts.explanation: When a Server Component renders a Client Component, the props cross a serialization boundary - they are turned into data the client can receive. So you can pass strings, numbers, plain objects/arrays, and even promises or Server Action references, but NOT arbitrary functions, class instances, or non-serializable values. Keep interactive logic on the client side of the boundary. For data, cache() (from react) wraps a function so that within a single server render, calling it with the same arguments returns a memoized result - useful when several components independently need the same data and you want to avoid duplicate fetches/queries. Together these shape efficient, correct server rendering with RSC.
concepts.analogy: The serialization boundary is like customs between two countries: only declared, packable goods (serializable data) cross. cache() is like a shared shopping receipt - if someone already bought the item this trip, others reuse it instead of buying again.
keyPoints: Props crossing server->client must be serializable.
Allowed: primitives, plain objects/arrays, promises, Server Action refs.
Not allowed: arbitrary functions, class instances, non-serializable values.
cache(fn) memoizes a data function for one server render.
Avoids duplicate fetches when multiple components need the same data.

lines: return db.users.find(id); // deduped within a render
// Two server components can both call getUser(42) and share one result:

async function Header() { const u = await getUser(42); return

{u.name}

; }

async function Sidebar() { const u = await getUser(42); return

{u.email}

; }
code.explanation: cache() ensures getUser(42) runs once per render even though Header and Sidebar both request it, deduplicating the query across the server tree.

commonMistakes: Passing a function or class instance as a prop into a Client Component.
Expecting cache() to persist across requests (it is per-render dedup, not a long-lived cache).
Putting interactive callbacks on the server side of the boundary.

exercises.prompt: Can you pass a regular onClick handler function from a Server Component to a Client Component as a prop?

solution.explanation: No - arbitrary functions are not serializable across the boundary. Define interactive handlers inside the Client Component. (Server Actions are a special serializable exception for server-side functions.)

checkYourself: What kinds of values can cross the serialization boundary?
What does cache() deduplicate, and over what scope?

concepts.title: Actions and form actions
concepts.summary: Actions are functions that handle data mutations, often async, with built-in support for pending states and errors. In React 19 you can pass a function to a <form action={fn}>, and React manages submission, pending UI, and resetting.
concepts.explanation: React 19 formalizes 'Actions' for handling submissions and mutations. Instead of wiring onSubmit, preventDefault, loading flags, and error state by hand, you pass a function to a form's action prop: <form action={handleSubmit}>. React calls it with the FormData on submit, manages a pending state during async work, and integrates with hooks like useActionState and useFormStatus. Actions can be client functions or Server Actions ("use server"). They reduce boilerplate for the common 'submit -> mutate -> show pending/result' flow and play well with optimistic updates and transitions.
concepts.analogy: A form action is like handing the whole order ticket to the kitchen and getting status updates back automatically, instead of you personally tracking each step (took order, cooking, served).
keyPoints: Actions handle (often async) mutations with built-in pending/error support.
<form action={fn}> lets React manage submission.
The action receives FormData; React tracks the pending state.
Works with useActionState and useFormStatus.
Can be client functions or Server Actions ("use server").

lines: await api.subscribe(email); // React tracks pending during this
code.explanation: Passing subscribe to action lets React call it with FormData on submit and manage the async pending state - no manual onSubmit/preventDefault/loading wiring.

commonMistakes: Mixing the new action prop with manual onSubmit/preventDefault unnecessarily.
Forgetting to give inputs name attributes (FormData reads by name).
Assuming form actions require a server (they can be client functions too).

exercises.prompt: Convert a manual onSubmit form to a React 19 action that reads a 'title' field and calls saveTitle.

solution.explanation: The action receives FormData; reading by the input's name avoids manual state, and React handles the pending state.

checkYourself: What does React manage for you when you use a form action?
How does the action read submitted field values?

concepts.title: useActionState
concepts.summary: useActionState runs an action and tracks its result and pending status in state. It returns the latest state, a wrapped action to pass to a form, and an isPending flag - ideal for form submissions with results or errors.
concepts.explanation: useActionState(actionFn, initialState) wires an action to component state. The actionFn receives the previous state and the form's input (e.g., FormData) and returns the next state (such as a success message or validation error). The hook returns [state, formAction, isPending]: state is the latest returned value, formAction is what you pass to <form action={...}>, and isPending indicates an in-flight submission. This centralizes the 'submit -> result/error -> render' loop without manual state and loading flags. It pairs naturally with Server Actions and progressive enhancement. (It was previously called useFormState during the canary phase.)
concepts.analogy: useActionState is like an order tracker that remembers your last order's outcome (delivered/failed) and shows a 'preparing' light while the next one is in progress.
keyPoints: useActionState(action, initialState) -> [state, formAction, isPending].
The action receives previous state + input and returns the next state.
state holds the latest result (message, error, data).
Pass formAction to <form action={...}>; isPending tracks submission.
Formerly useFormState during canary.

code.explanation: The action validates and subscribes, returning either an error or success. The hook exposes that state plus isPending and the formAction to pass to the form.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the action's first argument is the previous state.
Not returning a new state value from the action (the UI then shows nothing new).
Passing the raw action instead of the returned formAction to the form.

exercises.prompt: What three things does useActionState return?

solution.explanation: The latest state (the action's returned value), a formAction to pass to <form action={...}>, and an isPending boolean for the in-flight submission.

checkYourself: What arguments does the action passed to useActionState receive?
Which returned value do you give to the form's action prop?

concepts.title: useFormStatus
concepts.summary: useFormStatus reads the submission status of the nearest parent <form> from within a child component - exposing pending, the submitted data, and method - so reusable submit buttons can show a loading state without prop drilling.
concepts.explanation: When building a reusable submit button or status indicator, you want to know if the surrounding form is submitting, but you do not want to pass isPending down through props. useFormStatus() (imported from react-dom) returns information about the parent form's current submission: pending (whether a submit is in flight), data (the FormData being submitted), method, and the action. It must be called from a component rendered INSIDE the form (not the component that renders the form itself). This lets you create a generic <SubmitButton /> that disables and shows a spinner whenever its form is submitting, reusable across any form.
concepts.analogy: useFormStatus is like a status light wired into the form's circuit: any component plugged into the form can read 'submitting now' without someone manually telling it.
keyPoints: Reads the nearest parent form's submission status.
Returns pending, data, method, action.
Must be used in a component INSIDE the form, not the one rendering it.
Enables reusable submit buttons/indicators without prop drilling.
Imported from react-dom.

code.explanation: SubmitButton reads the parent form's pending state via useFormStatus and disables itself during submission - reusable in any form, with no isPending prop.

commonMistakes: Calling useFormStatus in the component that renders the form (it reads the PARENT form, so this returns no active status).
Importing it from 'react' instead of 'react-dom'.
Expecting it to report status for a form that is not an ancestor.

exercises.prompt: Where must useFormStatus be called to report a form's submission status?

solution.explanation: In a component rendered inside that <form> (a descendant). Calling it in the component that renders the form itself will not report that form's status - it reads the nearest parent form.

checkYourself: What does useFormStatus return?
Why must it be used inside the form?

concepts.title: useOptimistic
concepts.summary: useOptimistic lets you show an immediate, optimistic UI update while an async action is pending, then automatically reconcile with the real result (or revert) when it completes. It makes interfaces feel instant.
concepts.explanation: For actions like sending a message or liking a post, waiting for the server before updating the UI feels slow. useOptimistic(actualState, updateFn) returns an optimistic state plus a function to apply an optimistic value. You display the optimistic state immediately when the user acts; React keeps it until the underlying action resolves, then the real state takes over - and if the action fails, the optimistic value is discarded, reverting to reality. This gives snappy UX (the message appears at once) while remaining correct (it reconciles with the server). It is typically used together with Actions and transitions.
concepts.analogy: useOptimistic is like writing your reply on the whiteboard the instant you decide to send it, then confirming it once the post office acknowledges delivery - and erasing it if delivery fails.
keyPoints: Shows an immediate optimistic update during a pending action.
useOptimistic(state, updateFn) -> [optimisticState, addOptimistic].
Reconciles to real state when the action resolves.
Reverts automatically if the action fails.
Used with Actions/transitions for instant-feeling UX.

lines: await sendMessage(text);    // real send; list reconciles after
code.explanation: The new message appears instantly with a 'sending' marker via addOptimistic, then the real messages prop takes over once sendMessage resolves; a failure would revert the optimistic entry.

commonMistakes: Forgetting that optimistic state is temporary - the real state must update to persist it.
Not indicating the pending/optimistic status to the user.
Using it without an async action, where it provides no benefit.

exercises.prompt: What happens to the optimistic update if the underlying async action fails?

solution.explanation: It is discarded and the UI reverts to the actual state. Optimistic updates are temporary; they only persist if the real state ends up reflecting them.

checkYourself: What does useOptimistic return?
When does the optimistic value get replaced?

concepts.title: The use() hook for reading a promise or context
concepts.summary: use() reads the value of a resource - a promise (suspending until it resolves) or a context. Unlike other hooks, use() may be called conditionally and inside loops, making async and context reads more flexible.
concepts.explanation: use() is a new primitive for reading resources during render. Given a promise, it suspends the component until the promise resolves and then returns its value (integrating with Suspense and error boundaries for loading/error states). Given a context, it returns the context value like useContext. Its special property is that it is NOT bound by the usual top-level-only rule: you can call use() inside conditions and loops, which is impossible with useState/useContext. This makes it convenient for conditionally reading context or awaiting data. For promises, the promise should generally come from a cached or stable source (often the server or a cache), not be created fresh each render.
concepts.analogy: use() is like a universal 'unwrap this' tool: hand it a sealed package (promise) and it waits until it can open it, or hand it a broadcast channel (context) and it tells you the current value - and you are allowed to use it even inside an 'if'.
keyPoints: Reads a resource: a promise (suspends) or a context (returns value).
With a promise, integrates with Suspense + error boundaries.
With a context, behaves like useContext.
May be called conditionally and in loops (unlike other hooks).
Promises should come from a stable/cached source, not created each render.

lines: const comments = use(commentsPromise); // suspends until resolved
if (withTheme) theme = use(ThemeContext); // ok inside an if
code.explanation: Comments awaits a promise with use() and suspends until it resolves. Themed reads context conditionally - something useContext cannot do, but use() can.

commonMistakes: Creating the promise inline each render, causing repeated suspends.
Forgetting Suspense and error boundaries when use() reads a promise.
Assuming all hooks can be conditional - only use() has this flexibility.

exercises.prompt: Name one thing use() can do that useContext and useState cannot.

solution.explanation: Be called conditionally or inside a loop. use() is exempt from the top-level-only Rules of Hooks, so you can read a context or promise inside an if, unlike useContext/useState.

checkYourself: What two kinds of resources can use() read?
Why must the promise passed to use() be stable/cached?

concepts.title: ref as a prop (no forwardRef needed)
concepts.summary: In React 19, function components can receive ref as a normal prop, so you no longer need forwardRef to expose an inner DOM node to a parent. Just read props.ref and attach it.
concepts.explanation: Previously, refs did not pass through custom components like other props, requiring forwardRef to bridge a parent's ref to an inner element. React 19 makes ref an ordinary prop for function components: declare it in your props (or destructure { ref }) and attach it to the desired element. This removes the forwardRef wrapper for new components, simplifying reusable inputs and buttons. Existing forwardRef code keeps working, and you will still see it across older codebases, but new code can drop it. (Class components are unaffected; this applies to function components.)
concepts.analogy: It is like a phone system upgrade where every extension is now directly dialable - you no longer need an operator (forwardRef) to connect the call to the right desk.
keyPoints: React 19: function components can take ref as a normal prop.
forwardRef is no longer required for new components.
Read/destructure { ref } and attach it to an element.
Existing forwardRef code still works.
Applies to function components (classes unchanged).

code.explanation: TextInput simply destructures ref from props and attaches it - the parent's ref reaches the inner input without forwardRef.

commonMistakes: Adding forwardRef in new React 19 code where a ref prop suffices.
Expecting ref-as-prop to work in older React versions.
Forgetting to actually attach the received ref to an element.

exercises.prompt: Rewrite a forwardRef-based input as a React 19 component using ref as a prop.

solution.explanation: ref is read directly from props and attached - no forwardRef wrapper needed.

checkYourself: What does React 19 change about refs on function components?
Does old forwardRef code still work?

concepts.title: Document metadata: <title> and <meta> directly in components
concepts.summary: React 19 lets you render <title>, <meta>, and <link> tags anywhere in your component tree, and React automatically hoists them into the document <head>. This simplifies per-page metadata without a separate library.
concepts.explanation: Managing page titles and meta tags used to require libraries (like react-helmet) or framework-specific APIs. In React 19 you can place <title>, <meta name=...>, and certain <link> tags directly inside any component, and React moves them to the document head. This means a component can declare its own SEO/title metadata co-located with its content, and it works with streaming SSR. There are sensible rules (e.g., how duplicates and ordering are handled), but the headline is that document metadata becomes a normal part of rendering. It is especially convenient for route/page components that need distinct titles and descriptions.
concepts.analogy: It is like letting each chapter of a book set the running header at the top of the page automatically, instead of a separate index telling the printer what header to use.
keyPoints: Render <title>, <meta>, and supported <link> anywhere in the tree.
React hoists them into the document <head> automatically.
Co-locates metadata with the component that owns it.
Works with streaming SSR.
Reduces the need for metadata libraries.

code.explanation: The <title> and <meta> are written inside the component but React hoists them into <head>, giving this page its own title and description.

commonMistakes: Assuming this works in older React versions without a helper library.
Rendering conflicting titles in multiple components without considering precedence.
Putting non-metadata tags expecting them to hoist.

exercises.prompt: Where does React place a <title> you render inside a deep component in React 19?

solution.explanation: React hoists it into the document <head>, so the page title updates even though you wrote the tag inside a nested component.

checkYourself: Which metadata tags can React 19 hoist?
What problem does this remove from typical setups?

concepts.title: Preloading resources: preload, preinit, preconnect
concepts.summary: React 19 exposes resource-loading APIs (from react-dom) to hint the browser to fetch resources early: preconnect opens a connection, preload fetches a resource, and preinit fetches and evaluates it. They improve loading performance for scripts, styles, and fonts.
concepts.explanation: Loading performance often hinges on starting downloads early. React 19 provides functions you can call to express intent: preconnect(url) warms up a connection to an origin you will use soon; preload(url, options) fetches a resource (script, stylesheet, font, image) so it is cached before it is needed; preinit(url, options) goes further and fetches AND initializes (e.g., loads and runs a script, or loads and applies a stylesheet). React deduplicates these hints and integrates them with SSR so they can be emitted as early as possible. Use them for known critical resources (a font, a key script) to reduce waterfalls. They complement, not replace, normal loading.
concepts.analogy: These are like calling ahead before a road trip: preconnect dials the hotel to confirm a line is open, preload has them prepare your room (fetched, ready), and preinit checks you in so you can walk straight in (fetched and initialized).
keyPoints: preconnect: open a connection to an origin early.
preload: fetch a resource so it is cached before use.
preinit: fetch AND initialize/evaluate the resource.
Imported from react-dom; deduplicated and SSR-aware.
Use for known-critical scripts, styles, fonts to cut waterfalls.

code.explanation: App hints the browser to connect to the CDN, prefetch a font, and fetch-and-run a script early, so these resources are ready sooner when needed.

commonMistakes: Preloading everything, which can compete with critical resources.
Confusing preload (fetch only) with preinit (fetch and initialize).
Importing these from 'react' instead of 'react-dom'.

exercises.prompt: What is the difference between preload and preinit?

solution.explanation: preload only fetches and caches a resource for later use; preinit fetches AND initializes it (e.g., loads and executes a script, or loads and applies a stylesheet).

checkYourself: What does preconnect do versus preload?
From which package are these imported?

concepts.title: <Context> used directly as a provider
concepts.summary: In React 19 you can render a context object directly as the provider: <ThemeContext value={...}> instead of <ThemeContext.Provider value={...}>. It is a small ergonomic improvement.
concepts.explanation: Context previously required the .Provider sub-component: <MyContext.Provider value={v}>. React 19 lets you use the context itself as the provider component: <MyContext value={v}>...</MyContext>. The behavior is identical - it supplies the value to descendants - but the syntax is shorter and cleaner. The old .Provider form still works for compatibility. Consuming is unchanged (useContext or use()). This is purely a quality-of-life change, but you will see the new form in modern React 19 code.
concepts.analogy: It is like being able to write someone's first name instead of their full title every time - same person, less to type.
keyPoints: React 19: <Context value={...}> acts as the provider.
Equivalent to the older <Context.Provider value={...}>.
Old .Provider syntax still works.
Consuming is unchanged (useContext / use()).
Purely an ergonomic/syntactic improvement.

code.explanation: ThemeContext is used directly as the provider with a value prop - shorter than ThemeContext.Provider, with identical behavior.

commonMistakes: Expecting the new syntax in older React versions.
Thinking it changes behavior (it is only syntax).
Mixing up provider syntax with how you consume the context (still useContext/use).

exercises.prompt: Rewrite <UserContext.Provider value={user}>...</UserContext.Provider> in the React 19 style.

solution.explanation: The context object itself serves as the provider in React 19, with the same value prop.

checkYourself: What is the React 19 shorthand for a context provider?
Does it change runtime behavior?

concepts.title: React Compiler: auto-memoization that removes the need for useMemo/useCallback
concepts.summary: The React Compiler is a build-time tool that automatically memoizes components and values, so React skips unnecessary re-renders without you manually writing useMemo, useCallback, or React.memo. It analyzes your code and inserts the optimizations.
concepts.explanation: Manual memoization (useMemo/useCallback/React.memo) is error-prone and clutters code. The React Compiler analyzes your components at build time and automatically applies fine-grained memoization, caching values and stabilizing references where it is safe, so re-renders are minimized by default. It relies on your code following the Rules of React (pure components, no mutation during render) - if you break those rules, the compiler may skip optimizing that code. The practical promise: write straightforward components and get good performance without hand-tuning memoization. Adoption is incremental and opt-in via build configuration, and the manual hooks still exist for edge cases. Treat the compiler as evolving tooling and follow the official guidance for its current status and setup.
concepts.analogy: The React Compiler is like a smart compiler optimization pass: you write clear code, and it inserts the performance bookkeeping for you - the way a language compiler optimizes loops you wrote naively.
keyPoints: Build-time tool that auto-memoizes components and values.
Reduces or removes the need for manual useMemo/useCallback/React.memo.
Requires following the Rules of React (purity, no render-time mutation).
Opt-in via build configuration; adoption is incremental.
Manual memoization hooks remain for edge cases.

lines: // With the React Compiler, this can stay simple:
// The compiler can memoize 'visible' and stabilize 'handleClick'
code.explanation: Without the compiler you might wrap visible in useMemo and handleClick in useCallback. With the compiler, the optimization is inserted automatically when the code follows the Rules of React.

commonMistakes: Assuming the compiler fixes code that violates the Rules of React (it may skip it).
Believing it eliminates the need to understand memoization concepts.
Treating its API/status as fixed - verify current docs for setup and stability.

exercises.prompt: What must your components do for the React Compiler to safely optimize them?

solution.explanation: Follow the Rules of React: be pure, avoid mutating props/state during render, and use hooks correctly. Code that breaks these rules may be skipped by the compiler.

checkYourself: What does the React Compiler automate?
Why does it depend on the Rules of React?

concepts.title: New and experimental: useEffectEvent, <Activity>
concepts.summary: These are newer/experimental APIs. useEffectEvent extracts non-reactive logic from an effect so it always sees the latest props/state without being a dependency. <Activity> lets you pre-render or keep parts of the UI alive (hidden) without unmounting. Verify their status before relying on them.
concepts.explanation: Two emerging capabilities. useEffectEvent wraps a piece of logic used inside an effect that should always read the latest values but should NOT be a dependency that re-triggers the effect - solving the 'I want the newest prop in my effect callback without re-running the whole effect' problem (e.g., logging the current value on an interval without restarting it). <Activity> (evolved from the Offscreen idea) lets you render UI in a hidden/inactive mode - keeping its state and even pre-rendering it - so switching to it is instant, instead of unmounting and losing state. Both are experimental or evolving; treat them as not-yet-stable, check the official docs/release notes, and avoid depending on exact APIs in production until they ship.
concepts.analogy: useEffectEvent is like a hotline that always reaches the current on-call person without you having to re-dial when staff change. <Activity> is like keeping a stage set built behind the curtain so you can reveal it instantly instead of rebuilding it each time.
keyPoints: useEffectEvent: non-reactive logic in effects that reads latest values without being a dependency.
Solves restarting-the-effect-just-to-read-new-values problems.
<Activity>: keep UI alive/hidden or pre-render without unmounting (state preserved).
Both are experimental/evolving - not guaranteed stable.
Check official release notes before using in production.

lines: // EXPERIMENTAL - API may change; verify current docs.
// useEffectEvent: read latest value without re-running the effect
}, [roomId]); // theme is NOT a dependency, so changing it does not reconnect
code.explanation: onConnected always sees the current theme, but it is not an effect dependency, so the connection does not restart when theme changes - only roomId re-runs the effect. (Experimental API.)

commonMistakes: Relying on experimental APIs in production before they stabilize.
Treating useEffectEvent as a general escape from listing real dependencies.
Assuming <Activity>'s name/API is final - it has evolved and may change.

exercises.prompt: What problem does useEffectEvent address?

solution.explanation: It lets logic inside an effect always read the latest props/state without those values being effect dependencies - so the effect does not re-run (e.g., reconnect) just to access an updated value. (It is experimental.)

checkYourself: Why is useEffectEvent useful for effects that should not re-run on every value change?
What does <Activity> let you avoid when toggling UI?

concepts.title: Class components: render(), this.state, this.setState
concepts.summary: Before hooks, components were often ES6 classes extending React.Component. They hold state in this.state, update it with this.setState (which merges and is batched), and define UI in a render() method.
concepts.explanation: A class component extends React.Component and must implement render() returning JSX. State is an object on this.state, initialized in the constructor (or as a class field). You never assign this.state directly after init; you call this.setState(partial) which shallow-MERGES the partial into existing state and schedules a re-render (updates are batched and effectively asynchronous, so read prior state via the updater form this.setState(prev => ...)). Props are read via this.props. Methods used as handlers often need binding (or class-field arrow functions) so this is correct. You will encounter class components in older codebases; new code uses function components and hooks.
concepts.analogy: A class component is like an older car model with a manual gearbox (this, binding, lifecycle): fully capable, but more moving parts to operate than the automatic (hooks).
keyPoints: extends React.Component; implement render().
State lives in this.state, initialized in constructor/class field.
this.setState shallow-merges and batches updates (effectively async).
Use this.setState(prev => ...) when depending on previous state.
Handlers need binding or arrow class fields for correct this.

code.explanation: State is on this.state; setState with an updater safely increments; the arrow class field keeps this bound; render returns the UI.

commonMistakes: Mutating this.state directly instead of using this.setState.
Forgetting to bind methods, so this is undefined in handlers.
Expecting this.setState to be synchronous (it is batched/async).

exercises.prompt: this is undefined when the button is clicked. Fix the handler.

solution.explanation: An arrow class field binds this automatically; the updater form avoids stale state.

checkYourself: How does this.setState combine with existing state?
Why might a class method need binding?

concepts.title: Lifecycle methods: componentDidMount, componentDidUpdate, componentWillUnmount
concepts.summary: Class components use lifecycle methods to run code at specific times: componentDidMount after first render, componentDidUpdate after updates, and componentWillUnmount before removal. Hooks consolidate these into useEffect.
concepts.explanation: The three most-used lifecycle methods: componentDidMount() runs once after the component is first inserted into the DOM - ideal for initial fetching or subscriptions. componentDidUpdate(prevProps, prevState) runs after every re-render (except the first); you compare prev values to act only on specific changes (and must guard setState to avoid loops). componentWillUnmount() runs just before the component is removed - the place to clean up subscriptions, timers, and listeners. In function components, useEffect covers all three: setup runs after mount/updates (controlled by deps) and the returned cleanup runs on unmount and before re-runs. Knowing the mapping helps when reading or migrating class code.
concepts.analogy: Lifecycle methods are like event cues in a play: 'on entrance' (didMount), 'after each scene change' (didUpdate), and 'before exit' (willUnmount). useEffect bundles those cues into one configurable system.
keyPoints: componentDidMount: after first render (initial fetch/subscribe).
componentDidUpdate(prevProps, prevState): after updates; compare prev to act selectively.
componentWillUnmount: before removal (cleanup).
Guard setState in componentDidUpdate to avoid infinite loops.
useEffect (with deps + cleanup) replaces all three in function components.

lines: // useEffect(() => { const id = setInterval(...); return () => clearInterval(id); }, []);
code.explanation: didMount starts the interval; willUnmount clears it. The commented useEffect with cleanup does the same job in a function component.

commonMistakes: Calling setState in componentDidUpdate without a condition, causing loops.
Forgetting componentWillUnmount cleanup, leaking timers/subscriptions.
Splitting related setup and cleanup across distant lifecycle methods (hooks colocate them).

exercises.prompt: Which single Hook replaces componentDidMount, componentDidUpdate, and componentWillUnmount?

solution.explanation: useEffect. Its setup runs after mount and updates (per the dependency array) and its returned cleanup runs before re-runs and on unmount, covering all three lifecycle methods.

checkYourself: When does componentDidUpdate run, and what arguments does it receive?
Where do you clean up subscriptions in a class component?

concepts.title: PureComponent and shouldComponentUpdate
concepts.summary: shouldComponentUpdate(nextProps, nextState) lets a class component skip re-rendering by returning false. PureComponent implements it automatically with a shallow comparison of props and state - the class-era equivalent of React.memo.
concepts.explanation: By default a class component re-renders whenever its parent does. shouldComponentUpdate(nextProps, nextState) is a hook into that decision: return false to skip the update, true to proceed - useful for optimizing expensive components by comparing relevant values. Writing it by hand is tedious, so React.PureComponent provides a built-in shouldComponentUpdate that shallowly compares all props and state and skips rendering when nothing changed. This is the class analog of React.memo for function components. The same caveat applies: shallow comparison means new object/array/function references defeat it, so stable references matter.
concepts.analogy: shouldComponentUpdate is a gatekeeper deciding whether to redo the work; PureComponent is that gatekeeper pre-installed to wave through only genuine changes (shallow check).
keyPoints: shouldComponentUpdate(nextProps, nextState): return false to skip re-render.
PureComponent auto-implements it via shallow props/state comparison.
Class-era equivalent of React.memo.
Shallow comparison - new reference props defeat it.
Use to optimize expensive class components.

lines: return nextProps.value !== this.props.value; // skip if unchanged
code.explanation: Row decides manually whether to update; FastRow uses PureComponent's built-in shallow comparison to skip re-renders when props/state are unchanged.

commonMistakes: Returning the wrong boolean from shouldComponentUpdate and dropping needed updates.
Expecting PureComponent to deep-compare (it is shallow).
Passing fresh object/function props that defeat the shallow check.

exercises.prompt: What is the function-component equivalent of PureComponent?

solution.explanation: React.memo - it skips re-rendering a function component when its props are shallowly equal, just as PureComponent does for class components.

checkYourself: What does returning false from shouldComponentUpdate do?
What comparison does PureComponent perform?

concepts.title: Old refs (string and callback) and legacy context
concepts.summary: Older React used string refs (this.refs.name) and callback refs (ref={el => ...}) before useRef/createRef, and a legacy context API (childContextTypes/contextTypes) before the modern createContext. These are deprecated or discouraged but appear in old code.
concepts.explanation: Refs history: string refs like ref="input" then this.refs.input are legacy and deprecated due to several problems. Callback refs - ref={el => { this.input = el; }} - are still valid and useful (React calls the function with the node on mount and null on unmount), and predate createRef/useRef. Modern code uses createRef (classes) or useRef (functions). Context history: the legacy API required defining childContextTypes on a provider and contextTypes on consumers, which was clunky and had update-propagation issues; it was replaced by the modern createContext/Provider/useContext. When maintaining old code you may meet these patterns; prefer migrating to the modern equivalents.
concepts.analogy: These are like older wiring standards in a house: string refs are knob-and-tube (deprecated, replace it), callback refs are an older-but-serviceable system, and legacy context is outdated plumbing replaced by modern fittings (createContext).
keyPoints: String refs (this.refs.x) are legacy and deprecated.
Callback refs (ref={el => ...}) are valid; React passes the node, or null on unmount.
Modern refs: createRef (class) and useRef (function).
Legacy context (childContextTypes/contextTypes) is replaced by createContext.
Prefer migrating old patterns to modern equivalents.

lines: setInput = (el) => { this.input = el; }; // el is the node, or null on unmount
code.explanation: The callback ref stores the DOM node when mounted and null when unmounted. String refs are deprecated; modern code uses useRef/createRef.

commonMistakes: Writing new code with string refs.
Forgetting a callback ref receives null on unmount (guard before use).
Using legacy context in new code instead of createContext.

exercises.prompt: What value does a callback ref receive when the element unmounts?

solution.explanation: null. React calls the callback ref with the DOM node on mount and with null on unmount, so you should guard against null when using the stored reference.

checkYourself: Which ref style is deprecated, and which modern APIs replace it?
What replaced the legacy context API?

concepts.title: Why hooks replaced all of these
concepts.summary: Hooks were introduced to solve long-standing problems with classes: hard-to-reuse stateful logic, related code split across lifecycle methods, and the confusion of this and binding. They let function components hold state and effects with simpler, more composable code.
concepts.explanation: Classes worked but had recurring pain points. (1) Reusing stateful logic required patterns like HOCs and render props that led to wrapper nesting; hooks let you extract logic into custom hooks that compose cleanly. (2) Related logic was scattered: a single concern (e.g., a subscription) was split across componentDidMount and componentWillUnmount, while unrelated concerns were mixed in the same method; useEffect colocates setup with its cleanup and separates unrelated effects. (3) this and binding caused bugs and boilerplate; function components avoid this entirely. Hooks also enable the compiler and concurrent features to reason about code more easily. The result is less code, better reuse, and clearer data flow - which is why new React is hook-based, with classes kept only for legacy and error boundaries.
concepts.analogy: Switching from classes to hooks is like moving from a few large, rigid drawers (lifecycle methods) where related items are split apart, to modular labeled containers (custom hooks) you can compose and rearrange freely.
keyPoints: Hooks make stateful logic reusable via custom hooks (no HOC/render-prop nesting).
useEffect colocates related setup/cleanup; separates unrelated effects.
No this/binding confusion in function components.
Less boilerplate, clearer composition and data flow.
New React is hook-based; classes remain for legacy and error boundaries.

lines: // Class: subscription split across two lifecycle methods
// Hook: setup + cleanup colocated, and reusable
code.explanation: The hook keeps subscribe and unsubscribe together and is reusable across components - addressing the class problems of scattered logic and poor reuse.

commonMistakes: Assuming hooks are just syntax sugar rather than a different composition model.
Recreating class lifecycle thinking in effects instead of synchronization thinking.
Believing classes are removed - they remain for legacy and error boundaries.

exercises.prompt: Name two concrete problems with class components that hooks address.

solution.explanation: Hard-to-reuse stateful logic (hooks enable composable custom hooks instead of HOC/render-prop nesting) and related logic split across lifecycle methods (useEffect colocates setup with cleanup). Eliminating this/binding confusion is a third.

checkYourself: How do hooks improve reuse compared to HOCs/render props?
Why are classes still around at all?
